subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the order and degree, if defined, of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\cos x=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the order and degree, if defined, of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\cos x=0{/tex}
solution_text: The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , so its order is one. It is a polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and the highest power raised to {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is one, so its degree is one.
solution_html: The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, so its order is one. It is a polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and the highest power raised to {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is one, so its degree is one.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the order and degree, if defined, of the differential equation {tex}x y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the order and degree, if defined, of the differential equation {tex}x y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
solution_text: The highest order derivative present in the given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} , so its order is two. It is a polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and the highest power raised to {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is one, so its degree is one.
solution_html: The highest order derivative present in the given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}, so its order is two. It is a polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and the highest power raised to {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is one, so its degree is one.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the order and degree, if defined, of the differential equation {tex}y^{\prime \prime \prime}+y^{2}+e^{y^{\prime}}=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the order and degree, if defined, of the differential equation {tex}y^{\prime \prime \prime}+y^{2}+e^{y^{\prime}}=0{/tex}
solution_text: The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is y′′′, so its order is three. The given differential equation is not a polynomial equation in its derivatives and so its degree is not defined.
solution_html: The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is y′′′, so its order is three. The given differential equation is not a polynomial equation in its derivatives and so its degree is not defined.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function y = e -3x is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 6y = 0
question_html: Verify that the function y = e-3x is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 6y = 0
solution_text: Given function is y = e -3x Differentiating both sides of equation with respect to x , we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-3 e^{-3x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, differentiating (1) with respect to x, we have {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=9 e^{-3 x}{/tex} Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}, \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and y in the given differential equation, we get L.H.S. = 9.e -3x + (-3.e -3x ) - 6.e -3x = 9.e -3x - 9.e -3x = 0 = R.H.S. Therefore, the given function is a solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given function is y = e-3x
Differentiating both sides of equation with respect to x , we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-3 e^{-3x}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, differentiating (1) with respect to x, we have{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=9 e^{-3 x}{/tex}
Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}, \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and y in the given differential equation, we get
L.H.S. = 9.e-3x + (-3.e-3x) - 6.e-3x = 9.e-3x - 9.e-3x = 0 = R.H.S.
Therefore, the given function is a solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function y = a cos x + b sin x, where, a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0
question_html: Verify that the function y = a cos x + b sin x, where, a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0
solution_text: We have to show y = a cosx + b sinx ...(i) is a solution of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} + y = 0{/tex} ....(ii) Differentiating both sides of equation (i) with respect to x, successively, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -a sinx + b cosx {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -a cosx - b sinx Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} and y in equation (ii), we get L.H.S = (-a cosx - b sinx) + (a cosx + b sinx) = 0 = R.H.S. Therefore, the given function is a solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have to show y = a cosx + b sinx ...(i) is a solution of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} + y = 0{/tex} ....(ii)
Differentiating both sides of equation (i) with respect to x, successively, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -a sinx + b cosx
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -a cosx - b sinx
Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} and y in equation (ii), we get
L.H.S = (-a cosx - b sinx) + (a cosx + b sinx) = 0 = R.H.S.
Therefore, the given function is a solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+1}{2-y}{/tex} , (y {tex}\neq{/tex} 2)
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+1}{2-y}{/tex}, (y {tex}\neq{/tex} 2)
solution_text: We have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+1}{2-y}{/tex} ...(i) Separating the variables in equation (i), we get (2 - y) dy = (x + 1) dx ...(ii) Integrating both sides of equation (ii), we get {tex}\int{/tex} (2 - y) dy = {tex}\int{/tex} (x + 1) dx or 2y - {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + x + C 1 or x 2 + y 2 + 2x - 4y + 2 C 1 = 0 or x 2 + y 2 + 2x - 4y + C = 0, where C = 2C 1 Which is the general solution of equation (i)
solution_html:
We have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+1}{2-y}{/tex} ...(i)
Separating the variables in equation (i), we get
(2 - y) dy = (x + 1) dx ...(ii)
Integrating both sides of equation (ii), we get 
{tex}\int{/tex} (2 - y) dy = {tex}\int{/tex} (x + 1) dx
or 2y - {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + x + C1
or x2 + y2 + 2x - 4y + 2 C1 = 0
or x2 + y2 + 2x - 4y + C = 0, where C = 2C1
Which is the general solution of equation (i)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
solution_text: The given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Since 1 + y 2 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0, therefore separating the variables, the given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} ...(i) Integrating both sides of equation (i), we get {tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\int \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} or tan -1 y = tan -1 x + C which is the general solution of equation (i)
solution_html:
The given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
Since 1 + y2 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0, therefore separating the variables, the given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
Integrating both sides of equation (i), we get
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\int \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
or tan-1 y = tan-1x + C
which is the general solution of equation (i)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -4xy 2 given that y = 1, when x = 0
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= -4xy2 given that y = 1, when x = 0
solution_text: If y ≠ 0, the given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{y^{2}}{/tex} = -4x dx ...(i) Integrating both sides of equation (i), we get {tex}\int \frac{d y}{y^{2}}=-4 \int x d x{/tex} or {tex}-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} = -2x 2 + C or y = {tex}\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-\mathrm{C}}{/tex} ...(ii) Substituting y = 1 and x = 0 in equation (ii), we get, C = - 1 Now substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get the particular solution of the given differential equation as {tex}y=\frac{1}{2 x^{2}+1}{/tex}
solution_html:
If y ≠ 0, the given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{y^{2}}{/tex} = -4x dx ...(i)
Integrating both sides of equation (i), we get
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{y^{2}}=-4 \int x d x{/tex}
or {tex}-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} = -2x2 + C
or y = {tex}\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-\mathrm{C}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Substituting y = 1 and x = 0 in equation (ii), we get, C = - 1
Now substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get the particular solution of the given differential equation as {tex}y=\frac{1}{2 x^{2}+1}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the equation of the curve passing through the point (1, 1) whose differential equation is x dy = (2x 2 + 1) dx (x ≠ 0).
question_html: Find the equation of the curve passing through the point (1, 1) whose differential equation is x dy = (2x2 + 1) dx (x ≠ 0).
solution_text: The given differential equation can be expressed as {tex}d y^{}=\left(\frac{2 x^{2}+1}{x}\right) d x^{}{/tex} or {tex}d y=\left(2 x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} ...(i) Integrating both sides of equation (i), we get {tex}\int d y=\int\left(2 x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} or y = x 2 + log |x| + C ...(ii) Equation (ii) represents the family of solution curves of the given differential equation but we are interested in finding the equation of a particular member of the family which passes through the point (1, 1). Therefore substituting x = 1, y = 1 in equation (ii), we get C = 0. Now substituting the value of C in equation (ii) we get the equation of the required curve as y = x 2 + log |x|
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be expressed as {tex}d y^{}=\left(\frac{2 x^{2}+1}{x}\right) d x^{}{/tex}
or {tex}d y=\left(2 x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} ...(i)
Integrating both sides of equation (i), we get {tex}\int d y=\int\left(2 x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex}
or y = x2 + log |x| + C ...(ii)
Equation (ii) represents the family of solution curves of the given differential equation but we are interested in finding the equation of a particular member of the family which passes through the point (1, 1). Therefore substituting x = 1, y = 1 in equation (ii), we get C = 0.
Now substituting the value of C in equation (ii) we get the equation of the required curve as y = x2 + log |x|

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (-2, 3), given that the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{2 x}{y^{2}}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (-2, 3), given that the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{2 x}{y^{2}}{/tex}.
solution_text: We know that the slope of the tangent to a curve is given by {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} so, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{y^{2}}{/tex} ...(i) Separating the variables, equation (i) can be written as y 2 dy = 2x dx ...(ii) Integrating both sides of equation (ii), we get {tex}\int{/tex} y 2 dy = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x dx or {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} = x 2 + C ...(iii) Substituting x = -2, y = 3 in equation (iii), we get C = 5 Substituting the value of C in equation (iii), we get the equation of the required curve as {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x 2 + 5 or y = (3x 2 + 15) 1/3
solution_html:
We know that the slope of the tangent to a curve is given by {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
so, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{y^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
Separating the variables, equation (i) can be written as
y2dy = 2x dx ...(ii)
Integrating both sides of equation (ii), we get
{tex}\int{/tex} y2dy = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x dx
or {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} = x2 + C ...(iii)
Substituting x = -2, y = 3 in equation (iii), we get C = 5
Substituting the value of C in equation (iii), we get the equation of the required curve as
{tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x2 + 5 or y = (3x2 + 15)1/3

subject: Mathematics
question_text: In a bank, principal increases continuously at the rate of 5% per year. In how many years ₹ 1000 double itself?
question_html: In a bank, principal increases continuously at the rate of 5% per year. In how many years ₹ 1000 double itself?
solution_text: Let P be the principal at any time t. According to the given problem, {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}=\left(\frac{5}{100}\right) \times \mathrm{P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{20}{/tex} ...(i) separating the variables in equation (i), we get {tex}\frac{d p}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{d t}{20}{/tex} ...(ii) Integrating both sides of equation (ii), we get log P = {tex}\frac{t}{20}{/tex} + C 1 or P = {tex}e^{\frac{t}{20}} \cdot e^{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{/tex} or P = {tex}\mathrm{C} e^{\frac{t}{20}}{/tex} (where e C 1 = C) ...(iii) Now, P = 1000, when t = 0 Substituting the values of P and t in (iii), we get C = 1000. Therefore, equation (iii), gives P = 1000 e t/20 Let t years be the time required to double the principal. Then 2000 = 1000 e t/20 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 20 log e 2.
solution_html:
Let P be the principal at any time t. According to the given problem, {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}=\left(\frac{5}{100}\right) \times \mathrm{P}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{20}{/tex} ...(i)
separating the variables in equation (i), we get
{tex}\frac{d p}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{d t}{20}{/tex} ...(ii)
Integrating both sides of equation (ii), we get
log P = {tex}\frac{t}{20}{/tex} + C1
or P = {tex}e^{\frac{t}{20}} \cdot e^{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{/tex}
or P = {tex}\mathrm{C} e^{\frac{t}{20}}{/tex} (where eC1 = C) ...(iii)
Now, P = 1000, when t = 0
Substituting the values of P and t in (iii), we get C = 1000. Therefore, equation (iii), gives
P = 1000 et/20
Let t years be the time required to double the principal. Then 2000 = 1000 et/20 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 20 loge2.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}(x-y) \frac{d y}{d x}=x+2 y{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}(x-y) \frac{d y}{d x}=x+2 y{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: The given differential equation can be expressed as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}\mathrm{F}(x, y)=\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex} Now {tex}\mathrm{F}(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda(x+2 y)}{\lambda(x-y)}=\lambda^{0} \cdot f(x, y){/tex} Therefore, F(x, y) is a homogenous function of degree zero. So, the given differential equation is a homogenous differential equation. {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{1+\frac{2 y}{x}}{1-\frac{y}{x}}\right)=g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ......(ii) R.H.S. of differential equation (ii) is of the form {tex}g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} and so it is a homogeneous function of degree zero. Therefore, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation. To solve it we make the substitution y = vx ......(iii) Differentiating equation (iii) with respect to, x we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ......(iv) Substituting the value of y and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} in equation (i) we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} or {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}-v{/tex} or {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}+v+1}{1-v}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex} .....(v) Integrating both sides of equation (v), we get {tex}\int \frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1-3}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=-\log |x|+C_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v-\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=-\log |x|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|-\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}} d v=-\log |x|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|-\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=-\log |x|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|+\frac{1}{2} \log x^{2}=\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C_{1}{/tex} Replacing v by {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right|+\frac{1}{2} \log x^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+C_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right) x^{2}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+C_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\log \left|\left(y^{2}+x y+x^{2}\right)\right|=2 \sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+2 \mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\log \left|\left(x^{2}+x y+y^{2}\right)\right|=2 \sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+2 y}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+C{/tex} which is the general solution of the differential equation (i)
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be expressed as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex} .....(i)
Let {tex}\mathrm{F}(x, y)=\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex} 
Now {tex}\mathrm{F}(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda(x+2 y)}{\lambda(x-y)}=\lambda^{0} \cdot f(x, y){/tex} 
Therefore, F(x, y) is a homogenous function of degree zero. So, the given differential equation is a homogenous differential equation.
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{1+\frac{2 y}{x}}{1-\frac{y}{x}}\right)=g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ......(ii)
R.H.S. of differential equation (ii) is of the form {tex}g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} and so it is a homogeneous function of degree zero. Therefore, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation. To solve it we make the substitution
y = vx ......(iii)
Differentiating equation (iii) with respect to, x we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ......(iv)
Substituting the value of y and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} in equation (i) we get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} 
or {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}-v{/tex} 
or {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}+v+1}{1-v}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex}  .....(v)
Integrating both sides of equation (v), we get
{tex}\int \frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1-3}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=-\log |x|+C_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v-\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=-\log |x|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|-\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}} d v=-\log |x|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|-\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=-\log |x|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|+\frac{1}{2} \log x^{2}=\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C_{1}{/tex} 
Replacing v by {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right|+\frac{1}{2} \log x^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+C_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right) x^{2}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+C_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\log \left|\left(y^{2}+x y+x^{2}\right)\right|=2 \sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+2 \mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\log \left|\left(x^{2}+x y+y^{2}\right)\right|=2 \sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+2 y}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)+C{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the differential equation (i)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: Given differential equation is {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation because {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \cos v \left[ v + x \frac { d v } { d x } \right] = v x \cos v + x{/tex} {tex}v x \cos v + x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = v x \cos v + x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos v d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \cos v d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin v = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = \log | x | + c \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} This is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex}
which is a homogeneous differential equation because {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \cos v \left[ v + x \frac { d v } { d x } \right] = v x \cos v + x{/tex}
{tex}v x \cos v + x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = v x \cos v + x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos v d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \cos v d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin v = \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = \log | x | + c \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
This is the required solution of given differential equation.

question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x+\left(y-2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right) d y=0{/tex} is homogeneous and find its particular solution, given that, x = 0 when y = 1.
question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x+\left(y-2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right) d y=0{/tex} is homogeneous and find its particular solution, given that, x = 0 when y = 1.
solution_text: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y}{2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} ......(i) Let {tex}F(x, y)=\frac{2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y}{2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} Then, {tex}\mathrm{F}(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda\left(2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y\right)}{\lambda\left(2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)}=\lambda^{0}[\mathrm{F}(x, y)]{/tex} Thus, F(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero. Therefore, the given differential equation is a homogeneous differential equation. To solve it, we make the substitution x = vy ... (ii) Differentiating equation (ii) with respect to y, we get {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} Substituting the value of x and {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} in equation (i) we get {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} or {tex}y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} or {tex}y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} or {tex}2 e^{v} d v=\frac{-d y}{y}{/tex} or {tex}\int 2 e^{v} \cdot d v=-\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} or {tex}2 e^{v}=-\log |y|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} and replacing v by {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} , we get {tex}2 e^{\frac{x}{y}}+\log |y|=C{/tex} .......(iii) Substituting x = 0 and y = 1 in equation (iii), we get {tex}2 e^{0}+\log |1|=\mathrm{C} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=2{/tex} Substituting the value of C in equation (iii), we get {tex}2 e^{\frac{x}{y}}+\log |y|=2{/tex} which is the particular solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y}{2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} ......(i)
Let {tex}F(x, y)=\frac{2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y}{2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} 
Then, {tex}\mathrm{F}(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda\left(2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y\right)}{\lambda\left(2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)}=\lambda^{0}[\mathrm{F}(x, y)]{/tex} 
Thus, F(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero. Therefore, the given differential equation is a homogeneous differential equation. To solve it, we make the substitution
x = vy ... (ii)
Differentiating equation (ii) with respect to y, we get
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} 
Substituting the value of x and {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} in equation (i) we get
{tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} 
or {tex}y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} 
or {tex}y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} 
or {tex}2 e^{v} d v=\frac{-d y}{y}{/tex} 
or {tex}\int 2 e^{v} \cdot d v=-\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} 
or {tex}2 e^{v}=-\log |y|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
and replacing v by {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex}, we get
{tex}2 e^{\frac{x}{y}}+\log |y|=C{/tex}  .......(iii)
Substituting x = 0 and y = 1 in equation (iii), we get
{tex}2 e^{0}+\log |1|=\mathrm{C} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=2{/tex} 
Substituting the value of C in equation (iii), we get
{tex}2 e^{\frac{x}{y}}+\log |y|=2{/tex} 
which is the particular solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the family of curves for which the slope of the tangent at any point (x, y) on it is {tex} \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex} , is given by x 2 - y 2 = cx.
question_html: Show that the family of curves for which the slope of the tangent at any point (x, y) on it is {tex} \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex}, is given by x2 - y2 = cx.
solution_text: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex} Clearly, each of the function x 2 + y 2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, so the given equation is homogeneous. Put y = vx and {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} The given equation becomes {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { 2 v x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v } - v \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 - 2 v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 2 v } { v ^ { 2 } - 1 } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} [using variable separable form] On integrating both sides, we get -log|v 2 - 1| = log x - log C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow - \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| - \log x = - \log C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \right| = \log C _ { 1 } \Rightarrow x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = - C _ { 1 } x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = C x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because C = - C _ { 1 } \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex}
Clearly, each of the function x2 + y2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, so the given equation is homogeneous.
Put y = vx and {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
The given equation becomes
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { 2 v x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v } - v \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 - 2 v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 2 v } { v ^ { 2 } - 1 } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} [using variable separable form]
On integrating both sides, we get
-log|v2 - 1| = log x - log C1
{tex}\Rightarrow - \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| - \log x = - \log C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \right| = \log C _ { 1 } \Rightarrow x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = - C _ { 1 } x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = C x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because C = - C _ { 1 } \right]{/tex}

question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=\cos x{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=\cos x{/tex}
solution_text: Given differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} , where P = -1 and Q = cos x Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F}=e^{\int-1 d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of equation by I.F, we get {tex}e^{-x} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x} y=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d }{d x}\left(y e^{-x}\right)=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} On integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}y e^{-x}=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x+C{/tex} ......(i) Let {tex}I=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{e^{-x}}{-1}\right)-\int(-\sin x)\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\int \sin x e^{-x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\left[\sin x\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int \cos x\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}+\sin x e^{-x}-\int \cos x e^{-x} d x{/tex} or {tex}I=-e^{-x} \cos x+\sin x e^{-x}-I{/tex} or {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}{/tex} or {tex}I=\frac{(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}}{2}{/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get {tex}y e^{-x}=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right) e^{-x}+C{/tex} or {tex}y=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C} e^{x}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is of the form
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex}, where P = -1 and Q = cos x
Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F}=e^{\int-1 d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} 
Multiplying both sides of equation by I.F, we get
{tex}e^{-x} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x} y=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{d }{d x}\left(y e^{-x}\right)=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} 
On integrating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}y e^{-x}=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x+C{/tex} ......(i)
Let {tex}I=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{e^{-x}}{-1}\right)-\int(-\sin x)\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\int \sin x e^{-x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\left[\sin x\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int \cos x\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}+\sin x e^{-x}-\int \cos x e^{-x} d x{/tex} 
or {tex}I=-e^{-x} \cos x+\sin x e^{-x}-I{/tex} 
or {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}{/tex} 
or {tex}I=\frac{(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}}{2}{/tex} 
Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get
{tex}y e^{-x}=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right) e^{-x}+C{/tex} 
or {tex}y=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C} e^{x}{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}(x \neq 0){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}(x \neq 0){/tex}
solution_text: The given differential equation is {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}{/tex} ......(i) Dividing both sides of equation (i) by x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2}{x} y=x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} , where {tex}P=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} and Q = x. So, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2}{x} d x}=e^{2 \log x}=e^{\log x^{2}}=x^{2}\left[\text { as } e^{\log f(x)}=f(x)\right]{/tex} Therefore, solution of the given equation is given by {tex}y \cdot x^{2}=\int(x)\left(x^{2}\right) d x+\mathrm{C}=\int x^{3} d x+\mathrm{C}{/tex} or, {tex}y=\frac{x^{2}}{4}+C x^{-2}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is 
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}{/tex} ......(i)
Dividing both sides of equation (i) by x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2}{x} y=x{/tex} 
which is a linear differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex}, where {tex}P=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} and Q = x.
So, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2}{x} d x}=e^{2 \log x}=e^{\log x^{2}}=x^{2}\left[\text { as } e^{\log f(x)}=f(x)\right]{/tex} 
Therefore, solution of the given equation is given by
{tex}y \cdot x^{2}=\int(x)\left(x^{2}\right) d x+\mathrm{C}=\int x^{3} d x+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
or, {tex}y=\frac{x^{2}}{4}+C x^{-2}{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}y d x-\left(x+2 y^{2}\right) d y=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}y d x-\left(x+2 y^{2}\right) d y=0{/tex}
solution_text: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}=2 y{/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where {tex}P_{1}=-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q 1 = 2y. Therefore I.F = {tex}e^{\int-\frac{1}{y} d y}=e^{-\log y}=e^{\log (y)^{-1}}=\frac{1}{y}{/tex} Hence, the solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x \frac{1}{y}=\int(2 y)\left(\frac{1}{y}\right) d y+C{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x}{y}=\int(2 d y)+C{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x}{y}=2 y+C{/tex} which is a general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as 
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}=2 y{/tex} 
This is a linear differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where {tex}P_{1}=-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q1 = 2y.
Therefore I.F = {tex}e^{\int-\frac{1}{y} d y}=e^{-\log y}=e^{\log (y)^{-1}}=\frac{1}{y}{/tex} 
Hence, the solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}x \frac{1}{y}=\int(2 y)\left(\frac{1}{y}\right) d y+C{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{x}{y}=\int(2 d y)+C{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{x}{y}=2 y+C{/tex} 
which is a general solution of the given differential equation.

question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x {/tex} ( {tex} x \neq 0{/tex} ) given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x {/tex} ({tex} x \neq 0{/tex}) given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, P = cot x and Q = 2x + x 2 cot x. {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x = \int \left( 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x \right) \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + \int x ^ { 2 } \cos x d x + c{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \int 2 x \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x + C{/tex} ...(i) On putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}0 \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} On putting {tex} C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } cosec x{/tex} [divding both sides by sin x]
solution_html:
We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex}
This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}.
Here, P = cot x and Q = 2x + x2 cot x.
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x{/tex}
The general solution is given by
{tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x = \int \left( 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x \right) \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + \int x ^ { 2 } \cos x d x + c{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \int 2 x \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x + C{/tex} ...(i)
On putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}0 \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
On putting {tex} C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } cosec x{/tex}
[divding both sides by sin x]

question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, 1). If the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is equal to the sum of the x coordinate (abscissa) and the product of the x coordinate and y coordinate (ordinate) of that point.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, 1). If the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is equal to the sum of the x coordinate (abscissa) and the product of the x coordinate and y coordinate (ordinate) of that point.
solution_text: We know that the slope of the tangent to the curve is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} According to question, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x+x y{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-x y=x{/tex} ......(i) This is a linear differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} where P = -x and Q = x. Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int-x d x}=e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}{/tex} Hence, the solution of equation is given by {tex}y \cdot e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}=\int(x)\left(e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}\right) d x+C{/tex} ......(ii) Let {tex}I=\int(x) e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}} d x{/tex} Now, let {tex}\frac{-x^{2}}{2}=t{/tex} , then -x dx = dt or x dx = -dt Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I}=-\int e^{t} d t=-e^{t}=-e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}{/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get {tex}y e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}=-e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}+C{/tex} .....(iii) Now (iii) represents the equation of family of curves. But we are interested in finding a particular member of the family passing through (0, 1). Substituting x = 0 and y = 1 in equation (iii) we get 1 = -1 + C or C = 2 Substituting the value of C in equation (iii), we get {tex}y=-1+2 e^{\frac{x^{2}}{2}}{/tex} which is the equation of the required curve.
solution_html:
We know that the slope of the tangent to the curve is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} 
According to question, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x+x y{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-x y=x{/tex} ......(i)
This is a linear differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} where P = -x and Q = x.
Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int-x d x}=e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}{/tex} 
Hence, the solution of equation is given by
{tex}y \cdot e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}=\int(x)\left(e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}\right) d x+C{/tex} ......(ii)
Let {tex}I=\int(x) e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}} d x{/tex} 
Now, let {tex}\frac{-x^{2}}{2}=t{/tex}, then -x dx = dt or x dx = -dt
Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I}=-\int e^{t} d t=-e^{t}=-e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}{/tex} 
Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get
{tex}y e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}=-e^{\frac{-x^{2}}{2}}+C{/tex} .....(iii)
Now (iii) represents the equation of family of curves. But we are interested in finding a particular member of the family passing through (0, 1). Substituting x = 0 and y = 1 in equation (iii) we get
1 = -1 + C or C = 2
Substituting the value of C in equation (iii), we get
{tex}y=-1+2 e^{\frac{x^{2}}{2}}{/tex} 
which is the equation of the required curve.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function {tex}y=c_{1} e^{a x} \cos b x+c_{2} e^{a x} \sin b x{/tex} where c 1 , c 2 are arbitrary constants is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 a \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) y=0{/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function {tex}y=c_{1} e^{a x} \cos b x+c_{2} e^{a x} \sin b x{/tex} where c1, c2 are arbitrary constants is a solution of the differential equation  {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 a \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) y=0{/tex}
solution_text: The given function is y = e ax [c 1 cos bx + c 2 sin bx] ......(i) Differentiating both sides of equation (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[-b c_{1} \sin b x+b c_{2} \cos b x\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[c_{1} \cos b x+c_{2} \sin b x\right] e^{a x} \cdot a{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right) \cos b x+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right) \sin b x\right]{/tex} ......(ii) Differentiating both sides of equation (ii) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right)(-b \sin b x)+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right)(b \cos b x)\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right) \cos b x+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right) \sin b x\right] e^{a x} \cdot a{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[\left(a^{2} c_{2}-2 a b c_{1}-b^{2} c_{2}\right) \sin b x+\left(a^{2} c_{1}+2 a b c_{2}-b^{2} c_{1}\right) \cos b x\right]{/tex} Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}, \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and y in the given differential equation, we get L.H.S. = {tex}e^{a x}\left[a^{2} c_{2}-2 a b c_{1}-b^{2} c_{2}\right) \sin b x{/tex} + {tex}\left.\left(a^{2} c_{1}+2 a b c_{2}-b^{2} c_{1}\right) \cos b x\right]{/tex} - {tex}-2 a e^{a x}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right) \cos b x+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right) \sin b x\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) e^{a x}\left[c_{1} \cos b x+c_{2} \sin b x\right]{/tex} = e ax [ {tex}\left(a^{2} c_{2}-2 a b c_{1}-b^{2} c_{2}-2 a^{2} c_{2}+2 a b c_{1}+a^{2} c_{2}+b^{2} c_{2}\right) \sin b x{/tex} + {tex}\left(a^{2} c_{1}+2 a b c_{2}-b^{2} c_{1}-2 a b c_{2}-2 a^{2} c_{1}+a^{2} c_{1}+b^{2} c_{1}\right) \cos b x{/tex} ] = {tex}e^{a x}[0 \times \sin b x+0 \cos b x]{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x} \times 0=0{/tex} = R.H.S Hence, the given function is a solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given function is
y = eax [c1 cos bx + c2 sin bx] ......(i)
Differentiating both sides of equation (i) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[-b c_{1} \sin b x+b c_{2} \cos b x\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[c_{1} \cos b x+c_{2} \sin b x\right] e^{a x} \cdot a{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right) \cos b x+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right) \sin b x\right]{/tex} ......(ii)
Differentiating both sides of equation (ii) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right)(-b \sin b x)+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right)(b \cos b x)\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right) \cos b x+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right) \sin b x\right] e^{a x} \cdot a{/tex} 
= {tex}e^{a x}\left[\left(a^{2} c_{2}-2 a b c_{1}-b^{2} c_{2}\right) \sin b x+\left(a^{2} c_{1}+2 a b c_{2}-b^{2} c_{1}\right) \cos b x\right]{/tex} 
Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}, \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and y in the given differential equation, we get 
L.H.S. = {tex}e^{a x}\left[a^{2} c_{2}-2 a b c_{1}-b^{2} c_{2}\right) \sin b x{/tex} + {tex}\left.\left(a^{2} c_{1}+2 a b c_{2}-b^{2} c_{1}\right) \cos b x\right]{/tex} - {tex}-2 a e^{a x}\left[\left(b c_{2}+a c_{1}\right) \cos b x+\left(a c_{2}-b c_{1}\right) \sin b x\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) e^{a x}\left[c_{1} \cos b x+c_{2} \sin b x\right]{/tex} 
= eax[{tex}\left(a^{2} c_{2}-2 a b c_{1}-b^{2} c_{2}-2 a^{2} c_{2}+2 a b c_{1}+a^{2} c_{2}+b^{2} c_{2}\right) \sin b x{/tex} + {tex}\left(a^{2} c_{1}+2 a b c_{2}-b^{2} c_{1}-2 a b c_{2}-2 a^{2} c_{1}+a^{2} c_{1}+b^{2} c_{1}\right) \cos b x{/tex} ]
= {tex}e^{a x}[0 \times \sin b x+0 \cos b x]{/tex} = {tex}e^{a x} \times 0=0{/tex} = R.H.S
Hence, the given function is a solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
solution_text: According to the question,We have to find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} , given that y = 0, when x = 0. Now,we have, {tex}\log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x + 4 y }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m = n \Rightarrow e ^ { n } = m \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x } e ^ { 4 y }{/tex} On separating the variables, we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { e ^ { 4 y } } d y = e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int e ^ { - 4 y } d y = \int e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { - 4y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when x = 0. On putting y = 0 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 ( 0 ) } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 ( 0 ) } } { 3 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { - 0 } = e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 7 } { 12 }{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 12 } \Rightarrow 4 e ^ { 3 x } + 3 e ^ { - 4 y } - 7 = 0{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question,We have to find the particular solution of the differential equation
 {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} , given that y = 0, when x = 0.
Now,we have,
{tex}\log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x + 4 y }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m = n \Rightarrow e ^ { n } = m \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x } e ^ { 4 y }{/tex}
On separating the variables, we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { e ^ { 4 y } } d y = e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int e ^ { - 4 y } d y = \int e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { - 4y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 0, when x = 0.
On putting y = 0 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 ( 0 ) } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 ( 0 ) } } { 3 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { - 0 } = e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 7 } { 12 }{/tex}
On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 12 } \Rightarrow 4 e ^ { 3 x } + 3 e ^ { - 4 y } - 7 = 0{/tex}
which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}(x d y-y d x) y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = {tex}(y d x+x d y) x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}(x d y-y d x) y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = {tex}(y d x+x d y) x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
solution_text: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\left[x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d y{/tex} = {tex}\left[x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d x{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} Dividing numerator and denominator on RHS by x 2 , we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{y}{x} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right) \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\frac{y}{x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ......(i) Clearly, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} . To solve it, we make the substitution y = vx .....(ii) or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} or {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} (using (i) and (ii)) or {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v}\right) d v=\frac{2 d x}{x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int\left(\frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v}\right) d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} or {tex}\int \tan v d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} or {tex}\log |\sec v|-\log |v|=2 \log |x|+\log \left|C_{1}\right|{/tex} or {tex}\log \left|\frac{\sec v}{v x^{2}}\right|=\log \left|\mathrm{C}_{1}\right|{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\sec v}{v x^{2}}=\pm C_{1}{/tex} ......(iii) Replacing v by {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} in equation (iii), we get {tex}\frac{\sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\left(x^{2}\right)}=C{/tex} where, {tex}\mathrm{C}=\pm \mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\mathrm{C} x y{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\left[x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d y{/tex} = {tex}\left[x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d x{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} 
Dividing numerator and denominator on RHS by x2, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{y}{x} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right) \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\frac{y}{x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ......(i)
Clearly, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}. 
To solve it, we make the substitution
y = vx .....(ii)
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
or {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} (using (i) and (ii))
or {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} 
or {tex}\left(\frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v}\right) d v=\frac{2 d x}{x}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int\left(\frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v}\right) d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
or {tex}\int \tan v d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
or {tex}\log |\sec v|-\log |v|=2 \log |x|+\log \left|C_{1}\right|{/tex} 
or {tex}\log \left|\frac{\sec v}{v x^{2}}\right|=\log \left|\mathrm{C}_{1}\right|{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{\sec v}{v x^{2}}=\pm C_{1}{/tex} ......(iii)
Replacing v by {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} in equation (iii), we get
{tex}\frac{\sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\left(x^{2}\right)}=C{/tex} where, {tex}\mathrm{C}=\pm \mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} 
or {tex}\sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\mathrm{C} x y{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
solution_text: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i) Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii) Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} Substituting tan -1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get {tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i)
Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} 
where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} 
Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii)
Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} 
Substituting tan-1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex}, we get
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} 
or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} 
Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get 
{tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} 
or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

question_text: Determine order and degree (if defined) of differential equation y' + 5y = 0
question_html: Determine order and degree (if defined) of differential equation y' + 5y = 0
solution_text: It is given that equation is y’ + 5y = 0 We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is y’. Thus, its order is one. It is polynomial equation in y’. The highest power raised to y’ is 1. Therefore, its degree is one.
solution_html:
It is given that equation is y’ + 5y = 0
We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is y’.
Thus, its order is one. It is polynomial equation in y’. The highest power raised to y’ is 1.
Therefore, its degree is one.

question_text: Determine order and degree (if defined) of differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = cos 3x + sin 3x
question_html: Determine order and degree (if defined) of differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = cos 3x + sin 3x
solution_text: The given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = cos 3x + sin 3x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} - cos 3x - sin 3x = 0 We can see that the highest order derivative present in the given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} Thus, its order is two. It is polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d t^{2}}{/tex} and the power is 1 Therefore, its degree is one.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is  {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = cos 3x + sin 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} - cos 3x - sin 3x = 0
We can see that the highest order derivative present in the given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
Thus, its order is two.
It is polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d t^{2}}{/tex} and the power is 1
Therefore, its degree is one.

question_html: Determine order and degree (if defined) of differential equation {tex}\left(y^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)^{2}+\left(y^{\prime \prime}\right)^{3}+\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{4}+y^{5}=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that equation is (y”’) 2 + (y”) 3 +(y’) 4 + y 5 = 0 We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is y”’. Thus, its order is three. It is polynomial equation in y”’, y” and y’ So, the highest power raised to y”’ is 2 Therefore, its degree is two.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function y = e x + 1(explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y" - y' = 0
question_html: Verify that the function y = ex + 1(explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y" - y' = 0
solution_text: It is given that y = e x + 1 Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y^\prime=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x}\right)=e^x{/tex} ...(i) Now, Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(y^{\prime}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y'' = e^x=y'{/tex} ...[Using (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y'' = y'{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y'' - y'=0{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that y = ex + 1
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y^\prime=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x}\right)=e^x{/tex}   ...(i)
Now, Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(y^{\prime}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y'' = e^x=y'{/tex}    ...[Using (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y'' = y'{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y'' - y'=0{/tex}

question_text: Verify that the function y = x 2 + 2x + C (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y' - 2x - 2 = 0
question_html: Verify that the function y = x2 + 2x + C (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y' - 2x - 2 = 0
solution_text: Given: y = x 2 + 2x + C ...(i) To prove: y is a solution of the differential equation y' - 2x - 2 = 0 ...(ii) Proof:From, eq. (i), y' = 2x + 2 L.H.S. of eq. (ii), = y' - 2x - 2 = (2x + 2) - 2x - 2 = 2x + 2 - 2x - 2 = 0 = R.H.S. Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of y' - 2x - 2 = 0.
solution_html:
Given: y = x2 + 2x + C ...(i)
To prove: y is a solution of the differential equation y' - 2x - 2 = 0 ...(ii)
Proof:From, eq. (i),
y' = 2x + 2
L.H.S. of eq. (ii),
= y' - 2x - 2
= (2x + 2) - 2x - 2
= 2x + 2 - 2x - 2 = 0 = R.H.S.
Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of y' - 2x - 2 = 0.

question_html: Verify that the function y = cos x + C (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y' + sin x = 0.
solution_text: Given: y = cos x + C ....(i) To prove: y is a solution of the differential equation y' + sin x = 0 ...(ii) Proof: From eq. (i), y' = -sin x L.H.S. of eq. (ii), y' + sin x = -sin x + sin x = 0 = R.H.S. Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of y' + sin x = 0.

question_html: Verify that the function {tex}y = \sqrt {1 + {x^2}}{/tex} (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}y = \sqrt {1 + {x^2}} {/tex} …(i) To prove:y is a solution of the differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} …(ii) Proof: From eq. (i), {tex}y' = \frac{d}{{dx}}\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} = \frac{d}{{dx}}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^{1/2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^{ - 1/2}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^{ - 1/2}}.2x{/tex} {tex} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} }}{/tex} …(iii) Now R.H.S. of eq. (ii) {tex} = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{x}{{1 + {x^2}}}\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} {/tex} [From eq. (i)] {tex} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} }} = y'{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}y' = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \sqrt {1 + {x^2}} {/tex} …(i)
To prove:y is a solution of the differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} …(ii)
Proof: From eq. (i),
{tex}y' = \frac{d}{{dx}}\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} = \frac{d}{{dx}}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^{1/2}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^{ - 1/2}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^{ - 1/2}}.2x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} }}{/tex} …(iii)
Now R.H.S. of eq. (ii)
{tex} = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{x}{{1 + {x^2}}}\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} {/tex} [From eq. (i)]
{tex} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} }} = y'{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S
Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}y' = \frac{{xy}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}.

y' = A(1) = A

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function y = x sin x (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation {tex}xy' = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex} {tex}\left( {x \ne 0\,\,and\,\,x > y\,or\,x < - y\,} \right){/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function y = x sin x (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation {tex}xy' = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex} {tex}\left( {x \ne 0\,\,and\,\,x > y\,or\,x < - y\,} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Given: y = x sin x …(i) To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation {tex}xy' = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex} ...(ii) Proof: From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( { = y'} \right) = x\frac{d}{{dx}}\sin x + \sin x\frac{d}{{dx}}x{/tex} = x cos x + sin x L.H.S. of eq. (ii) = xy' = x(x cos x + sin x) = x 2 cos x + x sin x R.H.S. of eq. (ii) {tex} = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex} {tex} = x\sin x + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {x^2}{{\sin }^2}x} {/tex} [from eq. (i)] {tex} = x\sin x + x\sqrt {{x^2}\left( {1 - {{\sin }^2}x} \right)} {/tex} {tex}= x\sin x + x\sqrt {{x^2}{{\cos }^2}x} {/tex} {tex}= x\sin x + x.x\cos x{/tex} {tex}= x\sin x + {x^2}\cos x{/tex} {tex}= {x^2}\cos x + x\sin x{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}xy' = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex} .
solution_html:
Given: y = x sin x …(i)
To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation {tex}xy' = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex} ...(ii)
Proof: From eq. (i),
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( { = y'} \right) = x\frac{d}{{dx}}\sin x + \sin x\frac{d}{{dx}}x{/tex}
= x cos x + sin x
L.H.S. of eq. (ii)
= xy' = x(x cos x + sin x) = x2 cos x + x sin x
R.H.S. of eq. (ii)
{tex} = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = x\sin x + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {x^2}{{\sin }^2}x} {/tex} [from eq. (i)]
{tex} = x\sin x + x\sqrt {{x^2}\left( {1 - {{\sin }^2}x} \right)} {/tex}
{tex}= x\sin x + x\sqrt {{x^2}{{\cos }^2}x} {/tex}
{tex}= x\sin x + x.x\cos x{/tex}
{tex}= x\sin x + {x^2}\cos x{/tex}
{tex}= {x^2}\cos x + x\sin x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S
Hence, y given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}xy' = y + x\sqrt {{x^2} - {y^2}} {/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function xy = log y + C (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}\left( {xy \ne 1} \right){/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function xy = log y + C (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}\left( {xy \ne 1} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Given: xy = log y + C …(i) To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex} ....(ii) Proof: Differentiating both sides of eq. (i) w.r.t x, we have {tex}xy' + y\left( 1 \right) = \frac{1}{y}y' + 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow xy' - \frac{{y'}}{y} = - y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y'\left( {x - \frac{1}{y}} \right) = - y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y'\left( {\frac{{xy - 1}}{y}} \right) = - y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y'\left( {xy - 1} \right) = - {y^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y' = \frac{{ - {y^2}}}{{xy - 1}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y' = \frac{{ - {y^2}}}{{ - \left( {1 - xy} \right)}} = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex} Hence, function (implicit) given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}y' = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given: xy = log y + C …(i)

To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex} ....(ii)

Proof: Differentiating both sides of eq. (i) w.r.t x, we have

{tex}xy' + y\left( 1 \right) = \frac{1}{y}y' + 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow xy' - \frac{{y'}}{y} = - y{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y'\left( {x - \frac{1}{y}} \right) = - y{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y'\left( {\frac{{xy - 1}}{y}} \right) = - y{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y'\left( {xy - 1} \right) = - {y^2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y' = \frac{{ - {y^2}}}{{xy - 1}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y' = \frac{{ - {y^2}}}{{ - \left( {1 - xy} \right)}} = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex}

Hence, function (implicit) given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}y' = \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function y - cos y = x (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation (y sin y + cos y + x)y' = y
question_html: Verify that the function y - cos y = x (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation (y sin y + cos y + x)y' = y
solution_text: Given: y - cos y = x ....(i) To prove: y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation (y sin y + cosy + x)y' = y ....(ii) Proof: Differentiating both sides of eq. (i) w.r.t x, we have y' + (sin y)y' = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y'\left( {1 + \sin y} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y'=\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}}{/tex} ....(iii) Putting the value of x from eq. (i) and value of y' from eq. (iii) in L.H.S. of eq. (ii), (y sin y + cos y + x)y' {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {y\sin y + \cos y + y - \cos y} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y\sin y + y} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y\left( {\sin y + 1} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}} = y{/tex} = R.H.S. of (ii) Hence, Function given by eq. (i) is a solution of (y sin y + cos y + x)y' = y.
solution_html:
Given: y - cos y = x ....(i)

To prove: y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation

(y sin y + cosy + x)y' = y ....(ii)

Proof: Differentiating both sides of eq. (i) w.r.t x, we have

y' + (sin y)y' = 1

{tex}\Rightarrow y'\left( {1 + \sin y} \right) = 1{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y'=\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}}{/tex} ....(iii)

Putting the value of x from eq. (i) and value of y' from eq. (iii) in L.H.S. of eq. (ii),

(y sin y + cos y + x)y'

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {y\sin y + \cos y + y - \cos y} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y\sin y + y} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y\left( {\sin y + 1} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + \sin y}} = y{/tex} = R.H.S. of (ii)

Hence, Function given by eq. (i) is a solution of (y sin y + cos y + x)y' = y.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Verify that the function x + y = tan -1 y (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y 2 y' + y 2 + 1 = 0.
question_html: Verify that the function x + y = tan-1y (explicit or implicit) is a solution of differential equation y2y' + y2 + 1 = 0.
solution_text: Given: x + y = tan -1 y …(i) To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation y 2 y' + y 2 + 1 = 0 …(ii) Proof: Differentiating both sides of eq. (i) w.r.t x we have {tex}1 + y' = \frac{1}{{1 + {y^2}}}y'{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + y'} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right) = y'{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 + {y^2} + y' + y'{y^2} = y'{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y^2}y' + {y^2} + 1 = 0{/tex} Hence, function given by eq. (i) is a solution of y 2 y' + y 2 + 1 = 0
solution_html:
Given: x + y = tan-1y …(i)
To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation y2y' + y2 + 1 = 0 …(ii)
Proof: Differentiating both sides of eq. (i) w.r.t x we have
{tex}1 + y' = \frac{1}{{1 + {y^2}}}y'{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + y'} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right) = y'{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 + {y^2} + y' + y'{y^2} = y'{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {y^2}y' + {y^2} + 1 = 0{/tex}
Hence, function given by eq. (i) is a solution of y2y' + y2 + 1 = 0

solution_text: Given: {tex}y = \sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} , x\in \left( { - a,a} \right){/tex} …(i) To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation {tex}x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} …(ii) Proof: From eq. (i), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}\left( { - 2x} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - x}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}{/tex} …(iii) Putting the values of y and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} from eq. (i) and (iii) in L.H.S. of eq. (ii), {tex} = x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= x + \sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} \left( {\frac{{ - x}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}} \right){/tex} = x - x = 0 = R.H.S. of eq. (ii) Hence, function given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} , x\in \left( { - a,a} \right){/tex} …(i)
To prove:y given by eq. (i) is a solution of differential equation {tex}x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} …(ii)
Proof: From eq. (i),
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}\left( { - 2x} \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{ - x}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}{/tex} …(iii)
Putting the values of y and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} from eq. (i) and (iii) in L.H.S. of eq. (ii),
{tex} = x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}= x + \sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} \left( {\frac{{ - x}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}} \right){/tex}
= x - x = 0 = R.H.S. of eq. (ii)
Hence, function given by eq. (i) is a solution of {tex}x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}{/tex}
solution_text: Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow dy = \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}dx{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\int {dy = \int {\frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}dx} } {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \int {{{\tan }^2}\frac{x}{2}dx} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y= \int {\left( {{{\sec }^2}\frac{x}{2} - 1} \right)} dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y= \frac{{\tan \frac{x}{2}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} - x + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 2\tan \frac{x}{2} - x + c{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow dy = \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}dx{/tex}
Integrating both sides, {tex}\int {dy = \int {\frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}dx} } {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \int {{{\tan }^2}\frac{x}{2}dx} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y= \int {\left( {{{\sec }^2}\frac{x}{2} - 1} \right)} dx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y= \frac{{\tan \frac{x}{2}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} - x + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y = 2\tan \frac{x}{2} - x + c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{4-y^{2}}(-2<y<2){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{4-y^{2}}(-2<y<2){/tex}
solution_text: Here, we have {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{4-y^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\sqrt{4-y^{2}}}{/tex} = dx Integrating throughout, we get {tex} \int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\sqrt{4-\mathrm{y}^{2}}}=\int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}} d x=\sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}{/tex} = x + c {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{2}=\sin (x+c){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=2 \sin (x+c){/tex} {tex}\therefore ~y=2 \sin (x+c) {/tex} is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
Here, we have
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{4-y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\sqrt{4-y^{2}}}{/tex} = dx
Integrating throughout, we get
{tex} \int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\sqrt{4-\mathrm{y}^{2}}}=\int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}} d x=\sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}{/tex} = x + c
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{2}=\sin (x+c){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=2 \sin (x+c){/tex}
{tex}\therefore ~y=2 \sin (x+c) {/tex}is the general solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=1~(y \neq 1){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=1~(y \neq 1){/tex}
solution_text: Given equation is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = (1 - y) dx Separating variables {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{1-\mathrm{y}}{/tex} = dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{1-y}=\int d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y-1}=\int-d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log (y-1)=-x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y-1=e^{(-x+c)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y=e^{-x} \times e^{c}+1{/tex} Putting {tex}e^{c}=A​{/tex} {tex}y=A e^{-x}+1{/tex} is the general solution.
solution_html:
Given equation is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = (1 - y) dx
Separating variables
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{1-\mathrm{y}}{/tex} = dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{1-y}=\int d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y-1}=\int-d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log (y-1)=-x+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y-1=e^{(-x+c)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y=e^{-x} \times e^{c}+1{/tex}
Putting {tex}e^{c}=A​{/tex}
{tex}y=A e^{-x}+1{/tex} is the general solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation sec 2 x tan y dx + sec 2 y tan x dy = 0
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation sec2x tan y dx + sec2y tan x dy = 0
solution_text: Given Differential equation is, sec 2 x tan y dx + sec 2 y tan x dy = 0 Dividing by tan x tan y, we have {tex}\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\tan x}}dx + \frac{{{{\sec }^2}y}}{{\tan y}}dy = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\int\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\tan x}}dx + \int\frac{{{{\sec }^2}y}}{{\tan y}}dy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| {\tan x} \right| + \log \left| {\tan y} \right| = \log c{/tex} ... {tex}\left[ {\because \int {\frac{{f'\left( x \right)}}{{f\left( x \right)}}} dx = \log \left| {f\left( x \right)} \right|} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| {\tan x\tan y} \right| = \log c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\tan x\tan y} \right| = c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan x\tan y = \pm c = C{/tex} ... {tex}\left[ {\because \,\left| t \right| = a\left( {a \geqslant 0} \right) \Rightarrow t = \pm a} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given Differential equation is, sec2x tan y dx + sec2y tan x dy = 0
Dividing by tan x tan y, we have
{tex}\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\tan x}}dx + \frac{{{{\sec }^2}y}}{{\tan y}}dy = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\int\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\tan x}}dx + \int\frac{{{{\sec }^2}y}}{{\tan y}}dy = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| {\tan x} \right| + \log \left| {\tan y} \right| = \log c{/tex}    ...{tex}\left[ {\because \int {\frac{{f'\left( x \right)}}{{f\left( x \right)}}} dx = \log \left| {f\left( x \right)} \right|} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| {\tan x\tan y} \right| = \log c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\tan x\tan y} \right| = c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan x\tan y = \pm c = C{/tex}    ... {tex}\left[ {\because \,\left| t \right| = a\left( {a \geqslant 0} \right) \Rightarrow t = \pm a} \right]{/tex}
