question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( {{e^x} + {\text{ }}{e^{ - x}}} \right){\text{ }}dy{\text{ }} - {\text{ }}\left( {{e^x} - {\text{ }}{e^{ - x}}} \right){\text{ }}dx{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( {{e^x} + {\text{ }}{e^{ - x}}} \right){\text{ }}dy{\text{ }} - {\text{ }}\left( {{e^x} - {\text{ }}{e^{ - x}}} \right){\text{ }}dx{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0{/tex}

options.explanation: {tex}({e^x} + {e^{ - x}})dy = ({e^x} - {e^{ - x}})dx{/tex} {tex}\int {dy} = \int {\frac{{({e^x} - {e^{ - x}})}}{{({e^x} + {e^{ - x}})}}} dx {/tex} Since {tex}\int {\frac{{f'(x)}}{{f(x)}}} dx = \ ln f(x) +c{/tex} {tex}y = \log \left| {({e^x} + {e^{ - x}})} \right| + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow dy = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right)dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + {y^2}}} = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)dx{/tex} [Separating variables] Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{1}{{1 + {y^2}}}} dy = \int {\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}y = \frac{{{x^3}}}{3} + x + c{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right){/tex} 

{tex}\Rightarrow dy = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 + {y^2}} \right)dx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + {y^2}}} = \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)dx{/tex} [Separating variables]

Integrating both sides,

{tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{1}{{1 + {y^2}}}} dy = \int {\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} dx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}y = \frac{{{x^3}}}{3} + x + c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}y \log y d x-x d y=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}y \log y d x-x d y=0{/tex}
solution_text: Given Differential Equation is: y log y dx - x dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y log y) dx = xdy Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{x}=\frac{d y}{y \log y}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{ylogy}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log x +\log c=\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{ylogy}}{/tex} Let log y = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} d y=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log x + \log c=\int \frac{d t}{t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log x + log c = log t or {tex}\log (\log y)=\log x+\log c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log (\log y)=\log c x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \begin{aligned} &\log y=c x \Rightarrow y=e^{c x} \end{aligned}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given Differential Equation is: y log y dx - x dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y log y) dx = xdy
Separating variables,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{x}=\frac{d y}{y \log y}{/tex}
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{ylogy}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log x +\log c=\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{ylogy}}{/tex}
Let log y = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} d y=d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log x + \log c=\int \frac{d t}{t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log x + log c = log t
or {tex}\log (\log y)=\log x+\log c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log (\log y)=\log c x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \begin{aligned} &\log y=c x \Rightarrow y=e^{c x} \end{aligned}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}x^{5} \frac{d y}{d x}=-y^{5}{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}x^{5} \frac{d y}{d x}=-y^{5}{/tex}
solution_text: ​​​​​​Given differential equation is: {tex}x^{5} \frac{d y}{d x}=-y^{5}{/tex} Separating the variables we get {tex} \frac{d y}{y^{5}}=\frac{-d x}{x^{5}}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y^{5}}= -\int \frac{d x}{x^{5}}{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{y}^{-5} \mathrm{dy}= -\int \mathrm{x}^{-5} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{-4}}{-4}=-\frac{x^{-4}}{-4}+C_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-y^{-4}=x^{-4}+4 c_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{-4}+y^{-4}=c{/tex}
solution_html:
​​​​​​Given differential equation is: {tex}x^{5} \frac{d y}{d x}=-y^{5}{/tex}
Separating the variables we get
{tex} \frac{d y}{y^{5}}=\frac{-d x}{x^{5}}{/tex}
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y^{5}}= -\int \frac{d x}{x^{5}}{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{y}^{-5} \mathrm{dy}= -\int \mathrm{x}^{-5} \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y^{-4}}{-4}=-\frac{x^{-4}}{-4}+C_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-y^{-4}=x^{-4}+4 c_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{-4}+y^{-4}=c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}Given\: \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = sin -1 x dx Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int d y=\int \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex} Now to integrate sin -1 x we have to multiply it by 1 because, {tex}\left.\because(\left\{\int \mathrm{u} . \mathrm{v} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{u} \int \mathrm{v} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{u}\right)\left(\int \mathrm{vdx}\right) \mathrm{dx}\right\}\mathrm{}\right){/tex} So, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\int 1 . \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex} Let u be sin -1 x and v be 1 We can take the values of u and v from the formula (I.L.A.T.E) {tex}\therefore \mathrm{y}=\left\{\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x} \int 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)\left(\int 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}-\int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} let 1 - x 2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 2x dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xdx = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin -1 x + {tex}\int \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{t}} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int t^{-\frac{1}{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{t}+c{/tex} putting the value of t, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin -1 x + {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}Given\: \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
Separating variables,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dy = sin-1 x dx
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d y=\int \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex}
Now to integrate sin-1 x we have to multiply it by 1
because,
{tex}\left.\because(\left\{\int \mathrm{u} . \mathrm{v} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{u} \int \mathrm{v} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{u}\right)\left(\int \mathrm{vdx}\right) \mathrm{dx}\right\}\mathrm{}\right){/tex}
So,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\int 1 . \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex}
Let u be sin-1 x and v be 1
We can take the values of u and v from the formula (I.L.A.T.E)
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{y}=\left\{\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x} \int 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)\left(\int 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}-\int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} let 1 - x2 = t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 2x dx = dt
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xdx = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin-1 x + {tex}\int \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{t}} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int t^{-\frac{1}{2}} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{t}+c{/tex}
putting the value of t,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x sin-1 x + {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}{e^x}\tan ydx + \left( {1 - {e^x}} \right){\sec ^2}y\,dy = 0{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}{e^x}\tan ydx + \left( {1 - {e^x}} \right){\sec ^2}y\,dy = 0{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}{e^x}\tan ydx + \left( {1 - {e^x}} \right){\sec ^2}y\,dy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{e^x}\tan ydx = - \left( {1 - {e^x}} \right){\sec ^2}y\,dy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{{{e^x}}}{{1 - {e^x}}}dx = - \int {\frac{{{{\sec }^2}y}}{{\tan y}}} } dy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} - \log (1 - {e^x}) = - \log \tan y +\log c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\log \left( {\frac{{\tan y}}{{1 - {e^x}}}} \right) = \log c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{\tan y}}{{1 - {e^x}}} = c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\tan y = c(1 - {e^x}){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{e^x}\tan ydx + \left( {1 - {e^x}} \right){\sec ^2}y\,dy = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}{e^x}\tan ydx = - \left( {1 - {e^x}} \right){\sec ^2}y\,dy{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\int {\frac{{{e^x}}}{{1 - {e^x}}}dx = - \int {\frac{{{{\sec }^2}y}}{{\tan y}}} } dy{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} - \log (1 - {e^x}) = - \log \tan y +\log c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\log \left( {\frac{{\tan y}}{{1 - {e^x}}}} \right) = \log c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{\tan y}}{{1 - {e^x}}} = c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\tan y = c(1 - {e^x}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a solution of {tex} \left( x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 x ^ { 2 } + x{/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 1 when x = 0.
question_html: Find a solution of {tex} \left( x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 x ^ { 2 } + x{/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 1 when x = 0.
solution_text: According to the question, Given differential equation is , {tex} \left( x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 x^2 + x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad d y = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int d y = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 2 } ( x + 1 ) + 1 ( x + 1 ) } d x + C_2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } d x{/tex} ....(i) By Using partial fractions, {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } = \frac { A } { x + 1 } + \frac { B x + C } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} ...(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } = \frac { A \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) + ( B x + C ) ( x + 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } + x = A \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) + ( B x + C ) ( x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } + x = A \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) + B \left( x ^ { 2 } + x \right) + C ( x + 1 ){/tex} On comparing the coefficients of x 2 , x and constant terms from both sides, we get {tex}A + B = 2 \ ; \\B + C = 1{/tex} {tex}A+C = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A = -C On solving above equations, we get {tex} A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , B = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \text { and } C = \frac { - 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On substituting the values of A, B and C in Eq. (ii), we get {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { x + 1 } + \frac { \frac { 3 } { 2 } x - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} On integrating both sides w.r.t to x , we get {tex} \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex} + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \int \frac { x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + I _ { 1 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(iii) [from Eq. (1)] where {tex} I _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \int \frac { x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x{/tex} Put {tex} x ^ { 2 } + 1 = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t \Rightarrow x d x = \frac { d t } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \int \frac { d t } { t } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log | t | + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + C _ { 1 }{/tex} On putting the value of I 1 in Eq. (iii), we get {tex} y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex} [where, C = C 1 + C 2 ]
solution_html:
According to the question,
Given differential equation is , 
{tex} \left( x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 x^2 + x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad d y = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int d y = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { x ^ { 2 } ( x + 1 ) + 1 ( x + 1 ) } d x + C_2{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } d x{/tex} ....(i)
By Using partial fractions,
{tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } = \frac { A } { x + 1 } + \frac { B x + C } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } = \frac { A \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) + ( B x + C ) ( x + 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } + x = A \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) + ( B x + C ) ( x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 x ^ { 2 } + x = A \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) + B \left( x ^ { 2 } + x \right) + C ( x + 1 ){/tex}
On comparing the coefficients of x2, x and constant terms from both sides, we get
{tex}A + B = 2 \ ; \\B + C = 1{/tex}
{tex}A+C = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A = -C
On solving above equations, we get
{tex} A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , B = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \text { and } C = \frac { - 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
On substituting the values of A, B and C in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { x + 1 } + \frac { \frac { 3 } { 2 } x - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
On integrating both sides w.r.t to x , we get
{tex} \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + x } { ( x + 1 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex} + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \int \frac { x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + I _ { 1 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(iii) [from Eq. (1)]
where {tex} I _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \int \frac { x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x{/tex}
Put {tex} x ^ { 2 } + 1 = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t \Rightarrow x d x = \frac { d t } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \int \frac { d t } { t } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log | t | + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
On putting the value of I1 in Eq. (iii), we get
{tex} y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex}[where, C = C1 + C2]

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a solution of {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 , {/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 0 when x = 2.
question_html: Find a solution of {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 , {/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 0 when x = 2.
solution_text: We have differential equation, {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } = ( a - b ) ( a + b ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d y = \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int d y = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = I + K{/tex} ...(i) where, {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} By using partial fraction method, let {tex} \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x - 1 } + \frac { C } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + Bx(x + 1) + Cx(x - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x 2 - 1) + B(x 2 + x) + C(x 2 - x) Therefore, on comparing the coefficients of x 2 , x and constant terms from both sides, we get A + B + C = 0 B - C = 0 and -A = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -1 Therefore, on solving above equations, we get A = -1, {tex}B = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} , then {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x + 1 }{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}I = \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { - 1 } { x } d x + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 |{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of I in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + K{/tex} ....(ii) Also, we have, y = 0, when x = 2 Therefore,on putting y = 0 and x = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}0 = - \log 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3 + K{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \log \sqrt { 3 } \quad \quad [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of K in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
We have differential equation,
{tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } = ( a - b ) ( a + b ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad d y = \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int d y = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = I + K{/tex} ...(i)
where, {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
By using partial fraction method,
let {tex} \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x - 1 } + \frac { C } { x + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + Bx(x + 1) + Cx(x - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x2 - 1) + B(x2 + x) + C(x2 - x)
Therefore, on comparing the coefficients of x2, x and constant terms from both sides, we get
A + B + C = 0
B - C = 0
and -A = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -1
Therefore, on solving above equations, we get
A = -1, {tex}B = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex},
then {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x + 1 }{/tex}
Therefore,on integrating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}I = \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { - 1 } { x } d x + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 |{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the value of I in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + K{/tex} ....(ii)
Also, we have,  y = 0, when x = 2
Therefore,on putting y = 0 and x = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}0 = - \log 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3 + K{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \log \sqrt { 3 } \quad \quad [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the value of K in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_text: Find a solution of {tex}\cos \left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\;} \right) = a\;(a\; \in \;R){/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 1 when x = 0.
question_html: Find a solution of {tex}\cos \left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\;} \right) = a\;(a\; \in \;R){/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 1 when x = 0.

options.explanation: {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=cos^{-1}a {/tex} {tex} \int dy=cos^{-1}a\int dx {/tex} {tex} y=xcos^{-1}a+c{/tex} When y = 1 , x = 0 , then 1=0 {tex}cos^{-1}a+c{/tex} {tex}c=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=xcos^{-1}a+1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{y-1}{x}=cos^{-1}a{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a solution of {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\tan x{/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 1 when x = 0.
question_html: Find a solution of {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\tan x{/tex}  which satisfy the condition y = 1 when x = 0.
solution_text: Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\tan x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow dy = y\tan xdx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{y} = \tan xdx{/tex} [Separating variables] Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{1}{y}} dy = \int {\tan xdx} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| y \right| = \log \left| {\sec x} \right| + \log \left| c \right|{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| y \right| = \log \left| {c\sec x} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| y \right| = \left| {c\sec x} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \pm c\sec x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = C\sec x{/tex} where {tex} \pm c = C{/tex} …(i) Now putting y = 1 and x = 0 in eq. (i), we get 1 = C sec 0 = C Putting C = 1 in eq. (i), we get the required general solution {tex} \Rightarrow y = \sec x{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\tan x{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow dy = y\tan xdx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{y} = \tan xdx{/tex} [Separating variables]

Integrating both sides,

{tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{1}{y}} dy = \int {\tan xdx} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| y \right| = \log \left| {\sec x} \right| + \log \left| c \right|{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| y \right| = \log \left| {c\sec x} \right|{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left| y \right| = \left| {c\sec x} \right|{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \pm c\sec x{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = C\sec x{/tex} where {tex} \pm c = C{/tex}…(i)

Now putting y = 1 and x = 0 in eq. (i), we get 1 = C sec 0 = C

Putting C = 1 in eq. (i), we get the required general solution

{tex} \Rightarrow y = \sec x{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, 0) and whose differential equation is y′ = e x sin x.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, 0) and whose differential equation is y′ = ex sin x.

options.explanation: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {e^x}\sin x {/tex} {tex}\int {dy = \int {{e^x}} } \sin xdx {/tex} {tex} y = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\sin x - \cos x} \right){e^x} + C{/tex} When x = y = 0 , we get {tex}0 = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\sin 0 - \cos 0} \right){e^0} + C{/tex} {tex}\\C=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\sin x - \cos x} \right){e^x} + \frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex}2y-1=\left( {\sin x - \cos x} \right){e^x} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For the differential equation {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} , find the solution curve passing through the point (1, -1).
question_html: For the differential equation {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex}, find the solution curve passing through the point (1, -1).
solution_text: For this question, we need to find the particular solution at point(1, -1) for the given differential equation. Given differential equation is {tex}\Rightarrow x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{(x+2) d x}{x}{/tex} Or, {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{y}+2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\left(1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int d y-2 \int \frac{1}{y+2} d y=\int d x+2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-2 \log (y+2)=x+2 \log x+C{/tex} Now separating like terms on each side, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = 2 log x + 2 log (y + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log x 2 + log(y + 2) 2 Or, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 } .....(i) Now we are given that, the curve passes through (1, -1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 - 1 - c = log{1(-1 + 2) 2 } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 - c = log (1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2 + 0 ( {tex}\because{/tex} log(1) = 0) So c = -2 Putting the value of c in (i) y - x - (-2) = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 } y - x + 2 = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 }
solution_html:
For this question, we need to find the particular solution at point(1, -1) for the given differential equation.
Given differential equation is
{tex}\Rightarrow x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} 
Separating variables,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{(x+2) d x}{x}{/tex} 
Or,
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{y}+2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\left(1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d y-2 \int \frac{1}{y+2} d y=\int d x+2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y-2 \log (y+2)=x+2 \log x+C{/tex} 
Now separating like terms on each side,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = 2 log x + 2 log (y + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log x2 + log(y + 2)2
Or,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log{x2(y + 2)2} .....(i)
Now we are given that, the curve passes through (1, -1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-1 - 1 - c = log{1(-1 + 2)2}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 - c = log (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2 + 0 ({tex}\because{/tex} log(1) = 0)
So c = -2
Putting the value of c in (i)
y - x - (-2) = log{x2(y + 2)2}
y - x + 2 = log{x2(y + 2)2}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, –2) given that at any point (x, y) on the curve, the product of the slope of its tangent and y coordinate of the point is equal to the x coordinate of the point.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, –2) given that at any point (x, y) on the curve, the product of the slope of its tangent and y coordinate of the point is equal to the x coordinate of the point.

options.explanation: Given that {tex} y\frac{dy}{dx}=x {/tex} {tex}ydy=xdx{/tex} {tex}\int ydy=\int xdx{/tex} {tex}\\\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex} When x = 0 and y = 2, we get {tex}\frac{-2^{2}}{2}=\frac{0^{2}}{2}+c{/tex} c = 2 {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+2{/tex} {tex}y^{2}-x^{2}=4{/tex}

question_text: At any point (x, y) of a curve, the slope of the tangent is twice the slope of the line segment joining the point of contact to the point (– 4, –3). Find the equation of the curve given that it passes through (–2, 1).
question_html: At any point (x, y) of a curve, the slope of the tangent is twice the slope of the line segment joining the point of contact to the point (– 4, –3). Find the equation of the curve given that it passes through (–2, 1).

options.explanation: Given that {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=2\frac{y+3}{x+4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{y+3}=\frac{2dx}{x+4}{/tex} {tex}\\\int \frac{dy}{y+3}=\int \frac{2dx}{x+4}{/tex} {tex}\\log|y+3|=2log|x+4|+logc{/tex} Here, x= -2 and y = 1 {tex}log|1+3|=2log|-2+4|+logc{/tex} {tex}log4=log4+logc{/tex} {tex}logc=0{/tex} {tex}\log|y+3|=2log|x+4|{/tex} {tex}y+3=(x+4)^{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The volume of spherical balloon being inflated changes at a constant rate. If initially its radius is 3 units and after 3 seconds it is 6 units. Find the radius of balloon after t seconds.
question_html: The volume of spherical balloon being inflated changes at a constant rate. If initially its radius is 3 units and after 3 seconds it is 6 units. Find the radius of balloon after t seconds.
solution_text: Let the rate of change of the volume of the balloon be k. (k is a constant) {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t}=k{/tex} Or, {tex}\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right)=k{/tex} {Volume of sphere = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} } {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 3 r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{2} d r=k d t{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi \int r^{2} d r=k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4 \pi r^{3}}{3}=k t+c{/tex} .....(i) Now, given that At t = 0, r = 3 units, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4 \pi(3)^{3}}{3}=k(0)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi(3)^{2}=C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C=36 \pi{/tex} Now, at t = 3, r = 6 units: {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi \times (6)^{3}=3(\mathrm{k} \times 3+\mathrm{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}84 \pi{/tex} Substituting the values of k and c in (i) {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{3}=3(84 \pi t+36 \pi){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{3}=4 \pi(63 t+27){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r^{3}=63 t+27{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r=\sqrt[3]{63 t+27}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of balloon after t seconds is {tex}\sqrt[3]{63 t+27}{/tex} units.
solution_html:
Let the rate of change of the volume of the balloon be k. (k is a constant)
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t}=k{/tex} 
Or,
{tex}\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right)=k{/tex} {Volume of sphere = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} }
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 3 r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}=k{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{2} d r=k d t{/tex} 
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi \int r^{2} d r=k \int d t{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4 \pi r^{3}}{3}=k t+c{/tex} .....(i) 
Now, given that
At t = 0, r = 3 units,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4 \pi(3)^{3}}{3}=k(0)+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi(3)^{2}=C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow C=36 \pi{/tex} 
Now, at t = 3, r = 6 units:
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi \times (6)^{3}=3(\mathrm{k} \times 3+\mathrm{c}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}84 \pi{/tex}
Substituting the values of k and c in (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{3}=3(84 \pi t+36 \pi){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \pi r^{3}=4 \pi(63 t+27){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow r^{3}=63 t+27{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\sqrt[3]{63 t+27}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of balloon after t seconds is {tex}\sqrt[3]{63 t+27}{/tex} units.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: In a bank, the principal increases continuously at the rate of r% per year. Find the value of r if Rs.100 double itself in 10 years (log e 2 = 0.6931).
question_html: In a bank, the principal increases continuously at the rate of r% per year. Find the value of r if Rs.100 double itself in 10 years (loge2 = 0.6931).

options.explanation: Let P be the principal at any time t. then, {tex}\frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{{rP}}{{100}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{P}{{100}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int {\frac{1}{P}dP = \int {\frac{r}{{100}}dt} } {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log P = \frac{r}{{100}}t + \log c{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \frac{P}{c} = \frac{r}{{100}}t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P = c{e^{\frac{r}{{100}}}}{/tex} When P = 100 and t = 0., then, c = 100, Thus, we have: {tex}\Rightarrow P=100 \ {{e}^{{}^{r}\!\!\diagup\!\!{}_{100}\;}}{/tex} Now, suppose t = T, when P = 100., then; {tex}\Rightarrow 200 = 100{e^{\frac{T}{{100}}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {e^{\frac{T}{{100}}}} = 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow T = 100\log 2{/tex} = 100(0.6931) = 6.93%. Which is the required solution

subject: Mathematics
question_text: In a bank, principal increases continuously at the rate of 5% per year. An amount of ₹ 1000 is deposited with this bank, how much will it worth after 10 years {tex}\left( {{e^{0.5}} = 1.648} \right){/tex} .
question_html: In a bank, principal increases continuously at the rate of 5% per year. An amount of ₹ 1000 is deposited with this bank, how much will it worth after 10 years {tex}\left( {{e^{0.5}} = 1.648} \right){/tex}.
solution_text: Let P be the principal (amount) at the end of t years. According to the given condition, rate of increase of principal per year = 5% (of principal) {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{5}{{100}} \times P{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{P}{{20}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{P} = \frac{{dt}}{{20}}{/tex} [Separating variables] Integrating both sides, log P = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex} t + c ...(i) [Since P being principal > 0, hence {tex}\log \left| P \right| = \log P{/tex} ] Now initial principal = ₹ 1000 (given), i.e., when t = 0 then P = 1000 Therefore, putting t = 0, P = 1000 in eq. (i), log 1000 = c Putting {tex}\log 1000 = c{/tex} in eq. (i), log P = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex} t + log 1000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log P - log 1000 = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex} t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\frac{P}{1000}=\frac{1}{20} t{/tex} ...(ii) Now putting t = 10 years (given) {tex}\log \frac{P}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{20}} \times 10 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{P}{{1000}} = {e^{0.5}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} If x = t, then x = e t P = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.648 = ₹ 1648
solution_html:
Let P be the principal (amount) at the end of t years.
According to the given condition, rate of increase of principal per year = 5% (of principal)
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{5}{{100}} \times P{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{{dt}} = \frac{P}{{20}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dP}}{P} = \frac{{dt}}{{20}}{/tex} [Separating variables]
Integrating both sides,
log P = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex} t + c ...(i)
[Since P being principal > 0, hence {tex}\log \left| P \right| = \log P{/tex} ]
Now initial principal = ₹ 1000 (given), i.e., when t = 0 then P = 1000
Therefore, putting t = 0, P = 1000 in eq. (i), log 1000 = c
Putting {tex}\log 1000 = c{/tex} in eq. (i), log P = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex} t + log 1000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log P - log 1000 = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex}t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}log {tex}\frac{P}{1000}=\frac{1}{20} t{/tex} ...(ii)
Now putting t = 10 years (given)
{tex}\log \frac{P}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{20}} \times 10 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{P}{{1000}} = {e^{0.5}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} If x = t, then x = et
P = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.648 = ₹ 1648

subject: Mathematics
question_text: In a culture, the bacteria count is 1,00,000. The number is increased by 10% in 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach 2,00,000, if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present?
question_html: In a culture, the bacteria count is 1,00,000. The number is increased by 10% in 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach 2,00,000, if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present?
solution_text: Let y be the number of bacteria at any instant t. Given that the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t} \propto y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=k y{/tex} (k is a constant) Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{kdt}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + c.....(i) Let y’ be the number of bacteria at t = 0. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y’ = c Substituting the value of c in (i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + log y’ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log y’ = kt {tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{y}{y^{\prime}}=k t{/tex} .....(ii) Also, given that number of bacteria increases by 10% in 2 hours. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{110}{100} \mathrm{y}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}=\frac{11}{10}{/tex} ......(iii) Substituting this value in (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \times 2=\log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} So, (ii) becomes {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10} \times \mathrm{t}=\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} ......(iv) Now, let 't' be the time when the number of bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{y}^{\prime} \text { at } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}^{\prime}{/tex} So from (iv) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}^{\prime}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} So bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000 in {tex}\frac{2 \log 2}{\log _{\frac{11}{10}}}{/tex} hours.
solution_html:
Let y be the number of bacteria at any instant t.
Given that the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t} \propto y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=k y{/tex} (k is a constant)
Separating variables,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{kdt}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=k \int d t{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + c.....(i)
Let y’ be the number of bacteria at t = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y’ = c
Substituting the value of c in (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + log y’
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log y’ = kt
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{y}{y^{\prime}}=k t{/tex} .....(ii)
Also, given that number of bacteria increases by 10% in 2 hours.
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{110}{100} \mathrm{y}^{\prime}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}=\frac{11}{10}{/tex} ......(iii)
Substituting this value in (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \times 2=\log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} 
So, (ii) becomes
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10} \times \mathrm{t}=\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} ......(iv)
Now, let 't' be the time when the number of bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000.
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{y}^{\prime} \text { at } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}^{\prime}{/tex} 
So from (iv)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}^{\prime}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} 
So bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000 in {tex}\frac{2 \log 2}{\log _{\frac{11}{10}}}{/tex} hours.

question_text: The general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}{/tex} is
question_html: The general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}{/tex} is

options.explanation: We have, {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x} \times e^{y}{/tex} separating variables {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides {tex}\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}=\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-y}=e^{x}+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{-y}=-c{/tex} Or, {tex}e^{x}+e^{-y}=c{/tex} (c is a constant)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the differential equation of {tex}\left(x^{2}+x y\right) d y=\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of {tex}\left(x^{2}+x y\right) d y=\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: We have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} Here, putting x = kx and y = ky {tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{(k x)^{2}+(k y)^{2}}{(k x)^{2}+k x . k y}{/tex} = k 0 .f(x,y) Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. (x 2 + xy)dy = (x 2 + y 2 )dx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} To solve it we make the substitution. y = vx Differentiating above eq. with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+(v x)^{2}}{x^{2}+x \cdot v x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}\left(1+v^{2}\right)}{x^{2}(1+v)}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1+v}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}-\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1-v}{1+v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1+v}{1-v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating on both side, {tex}\int \frac{1+v}{1-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\int\left(-1+\frac{2}{1-v}\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} - v - 2log|1 - v| = log|x| + log c {tex}-\frac{y}{x}-2 \log \left|1-\frac{y}{x}\right|=\log |x|+\log C{/tex} {tex}-\frac{y}{x}=2 \log \left|1-\frac{y}{x}\right|+\log |x|+\log C{/tex} {tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x^{2}}+\log |x|+\log C{/tex} {tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x^{2}} \cdot C x{/tex} {tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x} c{/tex} {tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x} c{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})^{2}}{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y} / \mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})^{2}=\mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y} / \mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}(x-y)^{2}=k x e^{-y / x}{/tex} Which is the required solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} 
Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} 
Here, putting x = kx and y = ky
{tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{(k x)^{2}+(k y)^{2}}{(k x)^{2}+k x . k y}{/tex} = k0.f(x,y)
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
(x2 + xy)dy = (x2 + y2)dx 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}+x y}{/tex} 
To solve it we make the substitution.
y = vx
Differentiating above eq. with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+(v x)^{2}}{x^{2}+x \cdot v x}{/tex} 
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}\left(1+v^{2}\right)}{x^{2}(1+v)}{/tex} 
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1+v}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}-\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{v}}{/tex} 
{tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1-v}{1+v}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1+v}{1-v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
Integrating on both side,
{tex}\int \frac{1+v}{1-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\int\left(-1+\frac{2}{1-v}\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
- v - 2log|1 - v| = log|x| + log c 
{tex}-\frac{y}{x}-2 \log \left|1-\frac{y}{x}\right|=\log |x|+\log C{/tex} 
{tex}-\frac{y}{x}=2 \log \left|1-\frac{y}{x}\right|+\log |x|+\log C{/tex} 
{tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x^{2}}+\log |x|+\log C{/tex} 
{tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x^{2}} \cdot C x{/tex} 
{tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x} c{/tex} 
{tex}-\frac{y}{x}=\log \frac{(x-y)^{2}}{x} c{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})^{2}}{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y} / \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})^{2}=\mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y} / \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}(x-y)^{2}=k x e^{-y / x}{/tex} 
Which is the required solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the differential equation of {tex}y' = \frac{{x + y}}{x}{/tex} , is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of {tex}y' = \frac{{x + y}}{x}{/tex}, is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: Given: Differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{x + y}}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{x} + \frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 + \frac{y}{x} = f\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} …(i) Therefore, eq. (i) is homogeneous. Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x} = v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = vx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v.1 + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} Putting value of y and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} in eq. (i) {tex} \Rightarrow v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 1 + v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow xdv = dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow dv = \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} [Separating variables] Integrating both sides, {tex} \Rightarrow \int {1dv} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \log \left| x \right| + c{/tex} Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x} = v{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = \log \left| x \right| + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = x\log \left| x \right| + xc{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Differential equation {tex}y' = \frac{{x + y}}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{x} + \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 + \frac{y}{x} = f\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} …(i)
Therefore, eq. (i) is homogeneous.
Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x} = v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y = vx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v.1 + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}
Putting value of y and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} in eq. (i)
{tex} \Rightarrow v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 1 + v{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow xdv = dx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow dv = \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} [Separating variables]
Integrating both sides,
{tex} \Rightarrow \int {1dv} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v = \log \left| x \right| + c{/tex}
Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x} = v{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = \log \left| x \right| + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y = x\log \left| x \right| + xc{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the differential equation of {tex}(x-y) d y-(x+y) d x=0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of {tex}(x-y) d y-(x+y) d x=0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: We have (x - y)dy = (x + y)dx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} Here, putting x = kx and y = ky {tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{k x+k y}{k x-k y}{/tex} {tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} = k 0 .f(x,y) Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. (x - y) dy - (x + y) dx = 0 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} To solve it we make the substitution as follows , y = vx Differentiating above equation with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x+v x}{x-v x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}}{1-\mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{1-\mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides we get, {tex}\int \frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+v^{2}} d v-\int \frac{v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} ......(i) Let {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{v}{1+v^{2}} d v{/tex} Put {tex}1+v^{2}=t{/tex} 2vdv = dt {tex}\mathrm{vdv}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2} \log t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+\mathrm{v}^{2}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{v}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+\mathrm{v}^{2}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} from eq. (i)) {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right)=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\log x+\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(2 \log x+\log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)\right)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}} \times x^{2}\right)\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{2}\log (x^{2}+y^{2})+c{/tex} The required solution of the differential equation.
solution_html:
We have (x - y)dy = (x + y)dx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} 
Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex}
Here, putting x = kx and y = ky
{tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{k x+k y}{k x-k y}{/tex} 
{tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} 
= k0.f(x,y)
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
(x - y) dy - (x + y) dx = 0
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} 
To solve it we make the substitution as follows , 
y = vx
Differentiating above equation with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x+v x}{x-v x}{/tex} 
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}}{1-\mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{1-\mathrm{v}}{/tex} 
{tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
Integrating both sides we get,
{tex}\int \frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\int \frac{1}{1+v^{2}} d v-\int \frac{v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} ......(i)
Let {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{v}{1+v^{2}} d v{/tex} 
Put {tex}1+v^{2}=t{/tex} 
2vdv = dt
{tex}\mathrm{vdv}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2} \log t{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+\mathrm{v}^{2}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{v}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+\mathrm{v}^{2}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}  ({tex}\because{/tex} from eq. (i))
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right)=\log x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\log x+\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(2 \log x+\log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)\right)+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}} \times x^{2}\right)\right)+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{2}\log (x^{2}+y^{2})+c{/tex} 
The required solution of the differential equation.

question_text: Show that the differential equation of {tex}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x+2 x y d y=0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of {tex}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x+2 x y d y=0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: We have {tex}2 x y d y=-\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Let {tex}f(x, y)=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Here, putting x = kx and y = ky {tex}f(k x, k y)=-\frac{k^{2} x^{2}-k^{2} y^{2}}{2 k^{2} x y}{/tex} {tex}f(k x, k y)=-\frac{k^{2}}{k^{2}} \cdot \frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} = k 0 .f(x,y) Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. {tex}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x+2 x y d y=0{/tex} {tex}2 x y d y=-\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} To solve it we make the substitution. y = vx Differentiating eq. with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{v}^{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{vx}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}\left(1-\mathrm{v}^{2}\right)}{2 \mathrm{vx}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}-1}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1-v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} .......(i) Let {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v{/tex} Put 1 + v 2 = t 2vdv = dt {tex}\mathrm{vdv}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} = log(t) {tex}\therefore{/tex} log(1 + v 2 ) = -logx + logC ( {tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i) eq.) {tex}\log \left(1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right)=-\log x+\log c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}=C x{/tex} is the required solution of the differential equation.
solution_html:
We have 
{tex}2 x y d y=-\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} 
Let {tex}f(x, y)=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} 
Here, putting x = kx and y = ky
{tex}f(k x, k y)=-\frac{k^{2} x^{2}-k^{2} y^{2}}{2 k^{2} x y}{/tex} 
{tex}f(k x, k y)=-\frac{k^{2}}{k^{2}} \cdot \frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} 
= k0.f(x,y)
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
{tex}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x+2 x y d y=0{/tex} 
{tex}2 x y d y=-\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} 
To solve it we make the substitution.
y = vx
Differentiating eq. with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{v}^{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{vx}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}\left(1-\mathrm{v}^{2}\right)}{2 \mathrm{vx}^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}-1}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1-v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} 
{tex}-\frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} .......(i)
Let {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v{/tex} 
Put 1 + v2 = t
2vdv = dt
{tex}\mathrm{vdv}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} = log(t)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log(1 + v2) = -logx + logC ({tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i) eq.)
{tex}\log \left(1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right)=-\log x+\log c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}=C x{/tex}  is the required solution of the differential equation.

question_text: Show that the differential equation of {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: Clearly, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} Let, {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} Here, putting x = kx and y = ky {tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{k^{2} x^{2}-2 k^{2} y^{2}+k x k y}{k^{2} x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}f(k x, k y)= \frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} = k 0 .f(x,y) Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. Now, {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} To solve it we make the substitution. y = vx Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{x^{2}-2 v^{2} x^{2}+x \cdot v x}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}={1-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+\mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+\mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{1-2 v^{2}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1}{1-2 v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}or ~\int \frac{1}{1^{2}-(\sqrt{2} v)^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2.\sqrt{2}} \cdot \log \left|\frac{1+\sqrt{2} v}{1-\sqrt{2 v}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{1+\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}{1-\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{1+\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}{1-\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{x+\sqrt{2 y}}{x-\sqrt{2 y}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} Which is the required solution of the differential equation.
solution_html:
Clearly, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} 
Let, {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} 
Here, putting x = kx and y = ky
{tex}f(k x, k y)=\frac{k^{2} x^{2}-2 k^{2} y^{2}+k x k y}{k^{2} x^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}f(k x, k y)= \frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} 
= k0.f(x,y)
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
Now, {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}-2 y^{2}+x y}{x^{2}}{/tex} 
To solve it we make the substitution.
y = vx
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{x^{2}-2 v^{2} x^{2}+x \cdot v x}{x^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}={1-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+\mathrm{v}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+\mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{1-2 v^{2}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{1}{1-2 v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}or ~\int \frac{1}{1^{2}-(\sqrt{2} v)^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2.\sqrt{2}} \cdot \log \left|\frac{1+\sqrt{2} v}{1-\sqrt{2 v}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{1+\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}{1-\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{1+\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}{1-\sqrt{2} \frac{y}{x}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \log \left|\frac{x+\sqrt{2 y}}{x-\sqrt{2 y}}\right|=\log |x|+C{/tex} 
Which is the required solution of the differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the differential equation of xdy - ydx {tex}= \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} , is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of xdy - ydx {tex}= \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}, is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: Given differential equation is, {tex} x d y - y d x = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } \right) d x = x d y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} put, y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\because \int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {x^2}} }}} = \log \left| {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + {a^2}} } \right|and\int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} = \log |x|} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right| - \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{{\left| {\frac{{y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} }}{x}} \right|}}{x} = c{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { C } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { if } \log y = x } \ { \text { then } y = e ^ { x } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 } \cdot e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex} \therefore y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = A x ^ { 2 }{/tex} , where A = e C which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,
{tex} x d y - y d x = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } \right) d x = x d y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} 
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}
put, y = vx
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\because \int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {x^2}} }}} = \log \left| {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + {a^2}} } \right|and\int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} = \log |x|} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right| - \log | x | = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{{\left| {\frac{{y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} }}{x}} \right|}}{x} = c{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { C } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { if } \log y = x } \ { \text { then } y = e ^ { x } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 } \cdot e ^ { C }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = A x ^ { 2 }{/tex}, where A = eC
which is the required solution.

question_text: Show that the differential equation of {tex}\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y d x=\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x d y{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation of {tex}\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y d x=\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x d y{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y}{\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x}{/tex} Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y}{\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x}{/tex} Here, putting x = kx and y = ky {tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\left\{\mathrm{kx} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)+\mathrm{kysin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{ky}}{\left\{\mathrm{kysin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)-\mathrm{kx} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{kx}}{/tex} {tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{\mathrm{k}^{2}} \cdot \frac{\left\{\mathrm{x} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\mathrm{y} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{y}}{\left\{\mathrm{ysin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\mathrm{x} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} = k 0 .f(x,y) Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y}{\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x}{/tex} To solve it we make the substitution. y = vx Differentiating eq. with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\{\mathrm{x} \cos (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{vxsin}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{vx}}{\{\mathrm{vxsin}(\mathrm{v})-\mathrm{xcos}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\{\cos (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{v}}{\{\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})\}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\{\cos (\mathrm{v})+\operatorname{vsin}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{v}}{\{\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})\}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v} \cos (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{v}^{2} \sin (\mathrm{v})-\mathrm{v}^{2} \sin (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{v} \cos (\mathrm{v})}{\operatorname{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{v} \cos (\mathrm{v})}{\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v \sin (v)-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{\operatorname{vsin} \mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{v} \cos \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}-\frac{\cos \mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{v} \cos \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{tan vd} v-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \tan v d v-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \int \frac{1}{v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \sec v-\frac{1}{2} \log v=\log x+\log k{/tex} log sec v – log v = 2 log kx {tex}\log \sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=2 \log k x{/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}} \sec \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)=\log (\mathrm{kx})^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{y} \sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=k^{2} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}=k^{2}{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{xycos}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}C=\frac{1}{k^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{xycos}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c{/tex} The required solution of the differential equation.
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y}{\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x}{/tex} 
Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y}{\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x}{/tex} 
Here, putting x = kx and y = ky
{tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\left\{\mathrm{kx} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)+\mathrm{kysin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{ky}}{\left\{\mathrm{kysin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)-\mathrm{kx} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{kx}}{/tex} 
{tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{\mathrm{k}^{2}} \cdot \frac{\left\{\mathrm{x} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\mathrm{y} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{y}}{\left\{\mathrm{ysin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\mathrm{x} \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right\} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
= k0.f(x,y)
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left\{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} y}{\left\{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\} x}{/tex} 
To solve it we make the substitution.
y = vx
Differentiating eq. with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\{\mathrm{x} \cos (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{vxsin}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{vx}}{\{\mathrm{vxsin}(\mathrm{v})-\mathrm{xcos}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\{\cos (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{v}}{\{\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})\}}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\{\cos (\mathrm{v})+\operatorname{vsin}(\mathrm{v})\} \mathrm{v}}{\{\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})\}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v} \cos (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{v}^{2} \sin (\mathrm{v})-\mathrm{v}^{2} \sin (\mathrm{v})+\mathrm{v} \cos (\mathrm{v})}{\operatorname{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{v} \cos (\mathrm{v})}{\mathrm{vsin}(\mathrm{v})-\cos (\mathrm{v})}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{v \sin (v)-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\operatorname{vsin} \mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{v} \cos \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}-\frac{\cos \mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{v} \cos \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{tan vd} v-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \tan v d v-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \int \frac{1}{v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \sec v-\frac{1}{2} \log v=\log x+\log k{/tex} 
log sec v – log v = 2 log kx
{tex}\log \sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=2 \log k x{/tex} 
{tex}\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}} \sec \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)=\log (\mathrm{kx})^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{x}{y} \sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=k^{2} x^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}=k^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\operatorname{xycos}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}C=\frac{1}{k^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\operatorname{xycos}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c{/tex} 
The required solution of the differential equation.

question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y+x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y+x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
solution_text: We have {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}=y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}{/tex} Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}{/tex} Here, putting x = kx and y = ky {tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\mathrm{ky}-\mathrm{kx} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)}{\mathrm{kx}}{/tex} {tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{k}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{x} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} = k 0 .f(x,y) Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{xsin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}{/tex} To solve it we make the substitution. y = vx Differentiating eq. with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{vx}-\mathrm{xsin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\mathrm{x}} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}-\sin \mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\sin \mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sin v} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}cosec \ v dv =-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both side, we get {tex}\int cosec v d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} log(cosecv – cotv) = -logx + logC {tex}\log \left(cosec \frac{y}{x}-\cot \frac{y}{x}\right)=\log \frac{c}{x}{/tex} {tex}cosec \frac{y}{x}-\cot \frac{y}{x}=\frac{C}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sin \frac{y}{x}}-\frac{\cos \frac{y}{x}}{\sin \frac{y}{x}}=\frac{c}{x}{/tex} {tex}1-\cos \frac{y}{x}=\frac{C}{x} \cdot \sin \frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex}x\left(1-\cos \frac{y}{x}\right)=\operatorname{csin} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} The required solution of the differential equation.
solution_html:
We have
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}=y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}{/tex} 
Let {tex}f(x, y)=\frac{y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}{/tex} 
Here, putting x = kx and y = ky
{tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\mathrm{ky}-\mathrm{kx} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{ky}}{\mathrm{kx}}\right)}{\mathrm{kx}}{/tex} 
{tex}f(\mathrm{kx}, \mathrm{ky})=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{k}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{x} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
= k0.f(x,y)
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{xsin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x}{/tex} 
To solve it we make the substitution.
y = vx
Differentiating eq. with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{vx}-\mathrm{xsin}\left(\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\mathrm{x}} {/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}-\sin \mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\sin \mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{\sin v} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}cosec \ v dv =-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
Integrating both side, we get
{tex}\int cosec v d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
log(cosecv – cotv) = -logx + logC
{tex}\log \left(cosec \frac{y}{x}-\cot \frac{y}{x}\right)=\log \frac{c}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}cosec \frac{y}{x}-\cot \frac{y}{x}=\frac{C}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{\sin \frac{y}{x}}-\frac{\cos \frac{y}{x}}{\sin \frac{y}{x}}=\frac{c}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}1-\cos \frac{y}{x}=\frac{C}{x} \cdot \sin \frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
{tex}x\left(1-\cos \frac{y}{x}\right)=\operatorname{csin} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
The required solution of the differential equation.
