question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\sin x{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\sin x{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\sin x{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = 2 and Q = sin x) Now, I.F = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int 2 d x}=e^{2 x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F.}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\int \sin \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}I=\int \sin x \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\sin \mathrm{x} \int \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{ 2x }\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} = {tex}\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int\left(\cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} \int \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}(\cos \mathrm{x}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{2}-\int\left[(-\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{2}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \cos \mathrm{x}}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \int\left(\sin \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{4}(2 \sin x-\cos x)-\frac{1}{4} I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{4} \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{4}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{5}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x}){/tex} Now, putting the value of I in (i), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{5}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{5}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{Ce}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y=\frac{1}{5}(2 \sin x-\cos x)+C e^{-2 x}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\sin x{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = 2 and Q = sin x)
Now, I.F = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int 2 d x}=e^{2 x}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F.}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\int \sin \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} .....(i)
Let {tex}I=\int \sin x \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\sin \mathrm{x} \int \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{ 2x }\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int\left(\cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} \int \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}(\cos \mathrm{x}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{2}-\int\left[(-\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{2}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \cos \mathrm{x}}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \int\left(\sin \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{4}(2 \sin x-\cos x)-\frac{1}{4} I{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{4} \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{4}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{5}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x}){/tex} 
Now, putting the value of I in (i), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}}{5}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{5}(2 \sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{Ce}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}y=\frac{1}{5}(2 \sin x-\cos x)+C e^{-2 x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+3 y=e^{-2 x}{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+3 y=e^{-2 x}{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+3 y=e^{-2 x}{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = 3 and Q = e -2x ) Now, I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int 3 d x}=e^{3 x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}y(\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F})=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{3 \mathrm{x}}=\int\left(\mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{x}} \times \mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{3 \mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye 3x = e x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = e -2x + Ce -3x Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is y = e -2x + Ce -3x
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+3 y=e^{-2 x}{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = 3 and Q = e-2x)
Now, I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int 3 d x}=e^{3 x}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}y(\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F})=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{3 \mathrm{x}}=\int\left(\mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{x}} \times \mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{3 \mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye3x = ex + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = e-2x + Ce-3x
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is y = e-2x + Ce-3x

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=x^{2}{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=x^{2}{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=x^{2}{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = x 2 ) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}y(\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}(\mathrm{x})=\int\left(\mathrm{x}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x y=\int\left(x^{3}\right) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{4}}{4}+C{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x y=\frac{x^{4}}{4}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=x^{2}{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = x2)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}y(\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}(\mathrm{x})=\int\left(\mathrm{x}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x y=\int\left(x^{3}\right) d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{4}}{4}+C{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x y=\frac{x^{4}}{4}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \left( {\sec x} \right)y = \tan x\left( {0 \leq x < \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \left( {\sec x} \right)y = \tan x\left( {0 \leq x < \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \left( {\sec x} \right)y = \tan x{/tex} Comparing with {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex} , we have P = sec x and Q = tan x {tex}\therefore \int {Pdx = \int {\sec xdx = \log \left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)} } {/tex} {tex}I.F = {e^{\int {Pdx} }} = {e^{\log \left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)}} = \sec x + \tan x{/tex} Solution is {tex} = y\left( {I.F} \right) = \int {Q\left( {I.F} \right)} dx + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \int {\tan x\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)} dx + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \int {\left( {\sec x\tan x + {{\tan }^2}x} \right)} dx + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \int {\left( {\sec x.\tan x + {{\sec }^2}x - 1} \right)} dx + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \sec x + \tan x - x + c{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \left( {\sec x} \right)y = \tan x{/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex}, we have P = sec x and Q = tan x
{tex}\therefore \int {Pdx = \int {\sec xdx = \log \left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)} } {/tex}
{tex}I.F = {e^{\int {Pdx} }} = {e^{\log \left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)}} = \sec x + \tan x{/tex}
Solution is
{tex} = y\left( {I.F} \right) = \int {Q\left( {I.F} \right)} dx + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \int {\tan x\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)} dx + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \int {\left( {\sec x\tan x + {{\tan }^2}x} \right)} dx + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \int {\left( {\sec x.\tan x + {{\sec }^2}x - 1} \right)} dx + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right) = \sec x + \tan x - x + c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\tan x\left(0 \leq x<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\tan x\left(0 \leq x<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\cos ^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\tan x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\sec ^{2} x \cdot y=\sec ^{2} x \tan x{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = sec 2 x and Q = sec 2 x tan x) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^({^\int}^ \mathrm{(pdx)}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}y(I. F )=\int(Q \times I . F ) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ......(i) Now, Let t = tanx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 xdx = dt Thus, the equation (i) becomes, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\int\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \cdot \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\int\left(\mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}\right) \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{tanx}}=\mathrm{t} \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{t}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}\right) \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{tanx}}=\mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} te tanx = (t – 1)e t + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} te tanx = (tanx – 1)e tanx + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = (tanx -1) + C e -tanx Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is y = (tanx -1) + C e -tanx .
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\cos ^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\tan x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\sec ^{2} x \cdot y=\sec ^{2} x \tan x{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = sec2 x and Q = sec2 x tan x)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^({^\int}^ \mathrm{(pdx)}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}y(I. F )=\int(Q \times I . F ) d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ......(i)
Now, Let t = tanx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec2xdx = dt 
Thus, the equation (i) becomes,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\int\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \cdot \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\int\left(\mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}\right) \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{tanx}}=\mathrm{t} \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{t}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}\right) \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{tanx}}=\mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}-\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tetanx = (t – 1)et + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tetanx = (tanx – 1)etanx + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = (tanx -1) + C e-tanx 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
y = (tanx -1) + C e-tanx.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2} \log x{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2} \log x{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2} \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2}{x} y=x \log x{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{2}{x}{/tex} and Q = x log x) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{2(\log \mathrm{x})}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{x}^{2}}=\mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{x}^{2}=\int\left(\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\int\left(x^{3} \log x\right) d x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\log x . \int x^{3} d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int x^{3} d x\right] d x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\log x \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}-\int\left(\frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}\right) d x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\frac{x^{4} \log x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \int x^{3} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\frac{x^{4} \log x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\frac{1}{16} x^{4}(4 \log x-1)+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{16} \mathrm{x}^{2}(4 \log \mathrm{x}-1)+\mathrm{Cx}^{-2}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation {tex}y=\frac{1}{16} x^{2}(4 \log x-1)+C x^{-2}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2} \log x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2}{x} y=x \log x{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{2}{x}{/tex} and Q = x log x)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{2(\log \mathrm{x})}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{x}^{2}}=\mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{x}^{2}=\int\left(\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\int\left(x^{3} \log x\right) d x+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\log x . \int x^{3} d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int x^{3} d x\right] d x+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\log x \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}-\int\left(\frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}\right) d x+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\frac{x^{4} \log x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \int x^{3} d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\frac{x^{4} \log x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4}+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=\frac{1}{16} x^{4}(4 \log x-1)+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{16} \mathrm{x}^{2}(4 \log \mathrm{x}-1)+\mathrm{Cx}^{-2}{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation
{tex}y=\frac{1}{16} x^{2}(4 \log x-1)+C x^{-2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log x}=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} , This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} ) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{xlog} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log |\log \mathrm{x}|}=\log \mathrm{x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} . \log \mathrm{x}=\int\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ....(i) Now, {tex}\int\left[\frac{2}{x^{2}} \cdot \log x\right] d x=2 \int\left(\log x \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\log x . \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right\} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\log _{\mathrm{X}}\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x){/tex} Now, substituting the value in (i), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}\text { y. } \log x=-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log x}=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex},
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} )
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{xlog} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log |\log \mathrm{x}|}=\log \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} . \log \mathrm{x}=\int\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ....(i)
Now, {tex}\int\left[\frac{2}{x^{2}} \cdot \log x\right] d x=2 \int\left(\log x \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[\log x . \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right\} d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[\log _{\mathrm{X}}\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x){/tex} 
Now, substituting the value in (i), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}\text { y. } \log x=-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y+2 x y d x=\cot x d x(x \neq 0){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y+2 x y d x=\cot x d x(x \neq 0){/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y+2 x y d x=c o t x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where {tex}p=\frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} ) Now, I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x}=e^{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)}=1+x^{2}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\int\left[\frac{\cot x}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\int \cot \mathrm{x} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\log |\sin \mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\log |\sin x|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y+2 x y d x=c o t x d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where {tex}p=\frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex})
Now, I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x}=e^{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)}=1+x^{2}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\int\left[\frac{\cot x}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\int \cot \mathrm{x} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)=\log |\sin \mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\log |\sin x|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0 \ (x \neq 0){/tex} .
question_html: Find the general solution {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0 \ (x \neq 0){/tex}.
solution_text: Given differential equation is, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0{/tex} Above equation can be written as {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y (1 + x \cot x ) = x{/tex} On dividing both sides with x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 + x \cot x } { x } \right) = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) = 1{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , where {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x{/tex} and {tex} Q = 1.{/tex} we know that , {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) d x } = e ^ { \log | x | + \log \sin x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | x \sin x | } [ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x sin x {tex}y \times { IF } = \int ( Q \times {I F } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \times x \sin x = \int 1 \times x \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow yx\sin x = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot x \sin x = x \int \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}- \int \left( \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \sin x d x \right) d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x - \int 1 ( - \cos x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \int \cos x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \sin x + C{/tex} On dividing both sides by x sin x, we get {tex}y = \frac { - x \cos x + \sin x + C } { x \sin x }{/tex} {tex} \therefore y = - \cot x + \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { C } { x \sin x }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,
{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0{/tex}
Above equation can be written as
{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y (1 + x \cot x ) = x{/tex}
On dividing both sides with x, we get
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 + x \cot x } { x } \right) = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) = 1{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex},
where {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x{/tex} and {tex} Q = 1.{/tex}
we know that , 
{tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) d x } = e ^ { \log | x | + \log \sin x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log | x \sin x | } [ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x sin x
{tex}y \times { IF } = \int ( Q \times {I F } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \times x \sin x = \int 1 \times x \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow yx\sin x = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot x \sin x = x \int \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}- \int \left( \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \sin x d x \right) d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x - \int 1 ( - \cos x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \int \cos x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \sin x + C{/tex}
On dividing both sides by x sin x, we get
{tex}y = \frac { - x \cos x + \sin x + C } { x \sin x }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore y = - \cot x + \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { C } { x \sin x }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}(x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}(x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}(x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=x+y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-x=y{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex} (where, p = -1 and Q = y) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdy}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int-\mathrm{dy}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}x(I . F .)=\int(Q \times I . F .) d y+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\int\left[\mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{y} \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dy}}(\mathrm{y}) \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}\right] \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{y}\left(-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}\right)-\int\left(-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{y}}+\int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{y}}-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - y – 1 + Ce y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + 1 = Ce y Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is x + y + 1 = Ce y
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}(x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=x+y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-x=y{/tex}
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex}(where, p = -1 and Q = y)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdy}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int-\mathrm{dy}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}x(I . F .)=\int(Q \times I . F .) d y+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\int\left[\mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{y} \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dy}}(\mathrm{y}) \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}\right] \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{y}\left(-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}\right)-\int\left(-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{y}}+\int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{y}}-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - y – 1 + Cey
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + 1 = Cey
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
x + y + 1 = Cey

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}y d x+\left(x-y^{2}\right) d y=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}y d x+\left(x-y^{2}\right) d y=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\mathrm{ydx}+\left(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}^{2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ydx}=\left(\mathrm{y}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{dy}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\left(y^{2}-x\right)}{y}=y-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y}=y{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = y) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdy}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{y}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}x(I . F .)=\int(Q \times I . F .) d y+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{y}=\int[\mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{y}] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x . y=\int y^{2} d y+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \cdot y=\frac{y^{3}}{3}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x =\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{C}{y}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x =\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{c}{y}{/tex} .
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\mathrm{ydx}+\left(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}^{2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ydx}=\left(\mathrm{y}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{dy}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\left(y^{2}-x\right)}{y}=y-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y}=y{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = y)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdy}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{y}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}x(I . F .)=\int(Q \times I . F .) d y+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{y}=\int[\mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{y}] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x . y=\int y^{2} d y+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x \cdot y=\frac{y^{3}}{3}+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x =\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{C}{y}{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x =\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{c}{y}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\left(x+3 y^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y(y>0){/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\left(x+3 y^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y(y>0){/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\left(x+3 y^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x+3 y^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x+3 y^{2}}{y}=\frac{x}{y}+3 y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}=3 y{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex} (where, p = - {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = 3y) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdy}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\log \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}\right)}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}x(I. F )=\int(Q \times I . F ) d y+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}=\int\left[3 \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}\right] \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=3 y+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3y 2 + Cy Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is x = 3y 2 + Cy.
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\left(x+3 y^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x+3 y^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x+3 y^{2}}{y}=\frac{x}{y}+3 y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}=3 y{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex} (where, p = -{tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = 3y)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdy}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\log \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}\right)}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}x(I. F )=\int(Q \times I . F ) d y+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}=\int\left[3 \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{y}}\right] \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=3 y+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3y2 + Cy
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is x = 3y2 + Cy.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2y tan x = sin x, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ 2y tan x = sin x, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}.
solution_text: According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2ytanx = sinx Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex} {tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex} The solution of differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} Dividing with sec 2 x on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cosx + C.cos 2 x According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} Now, y = cosx + C.cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ 2ytanx = sinx
Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}+ Py = Q
Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx
Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex}
{tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex}
The solution of differential equation is given by
{tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex}
 Dividing with sec2x on both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}y = cosx + C.cos2x
According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex}
Now, y = cosx + C.cos2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos2x
Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos2x

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} , given that y = 0 when x = 1.
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}, given that y = 0 when x = 1.
solution_text: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Divide both sides by 1 + x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex} Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Finding Integrating factor: IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Let 1 + x 2 = t Diff. w.r.t. x 2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex} Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex} IF = t IF = 1 + x 2 Solution of the differential equation: y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex} Putting values, y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} y (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx y (1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C ...(1) Putting that y = 0 and x = 1, 0(1 + 1 2 ) = tan -1 (1) + C 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C C = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Putting value of C in eq(1), y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
Divide both sides by 1 + x2
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q,
P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
Finding Integrating factor:
IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex}
IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
Let 1 + x2 = t
Diff. w.r.t. x
2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex}
dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex}
Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex}
IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex}
IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex}
IF = t
IF = 1 + x2
Solution of the differential equation:
y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex}
Putting values,
y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x2) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} 
y (1 + x2) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx
y (1 + x2) = tan-1x + C ...(1)
Putting that y = 0 and x = 1,
0(1 + 12) = tan-1(1) + C
0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C
C = -{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Putting value of C in eq(1),
y(1 + x2) = tan-1x + C
y(1 + x2) = tan-1x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} , given that y = 2 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} , given that y = 2 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
solution_text: Given, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i) This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin 2 x + C sin 3 x ...(ii) Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4 {tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,  {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i)
This is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt  in  Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin2 x + C sin3 x ...(ii)
Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get,
{tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4
{tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the origin given that the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is equal to the sum of the coordinates of the point.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the origin given that the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is equal to the sum of the coordinates of the point.
solution_text: Let F(x,y) be the curve passing through origin and let (x,y) be a point on the curve. We know the slope of the tangent to the curve at (x,y) is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} . According to the given conditions, we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x+y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = -1 and Q = x) Now, {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int(-1) d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ......(i) Now, {tex}\int \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{x} \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} = {tex}-x\left(e^{-x}\right)-\int\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right)+\left(-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} = {tex}-e^{-x}(x+1){/tex} Thus, from equation (i), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{x}+1)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -(x+1) + Ce x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + 1 = Ce x .......(ii) Now, it is given that curve passes through origin. Thus, equation (ii) becomes: 1 = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Substituting C = 1 in equation (ii), we get, x + y + 1 = e x Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is x + y + 1 = e x
solution_html:
Let F(x,y) be the curve passing through origin and let (x,y) be a point on the curve.
We know the slope of the tangent to the curve at (x,y) is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
According to the given conditions, we get,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x+y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = -1 and Q = x)
Now, {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int(-1) d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F} .) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ......(i)
Now, {tex}\int \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{x} \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
= {tex}-x\left(e^{-x}\right)-\int\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right)+\left(-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}-e^{-x}(x+1){/tex}
Thus, from equation (i), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{x}+1)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -(x+1) + Cex 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + 1 = Cex .......(ii)
Now, it is given that curve passes through origin.
Thus, equation (ii) becomes:
1 = C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Substituting C = 1 in equation (ii), we get,
x + y + 1 = ex 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
x + y + 1 = ex

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, 2) given that the sum of the coordinates of any point on the curve exceeds the magnitude of the slope of the tangent to the curve at that point by 5.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (0, 2) given that the sum of the coordinates of any point on the curve exceeds the magnitude of the slope of the tangent to the curve at that point by 5.
solution_text: Let F(x, y) be the curve and let (x, y) be a point on the curve. We know the slope of the tangent to the curve at (x, y) is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} According to the given conditions, we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+5=x+y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x-5{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = -1 and Q = x - 5) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int(-1) \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F.}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=\int(\mathrm{x}-5) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\int(\mathrm{x}-5) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=(\mathrm{x}-5) \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}-5) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}\right] \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}(x-5)\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}(x-5)\left(-e^{-x}\right)+\left(-e^{-x}\right){/tex} = {tex}(4-x) e^{-x}{/tex} Thus, from equation (i), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=(4-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 - x + Ce x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y - 4 = Ce x Now, it is given that curve passes through (0, 2). Thus, equation (ii) becomes: 0 + 2 - 4 = C e 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 2 = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -2 Substituting C = -2 in equation (ii), we get, x + y - 4 =-2e x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 - x - 2e x Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is y = 4 - x - 2e x
solution_html:
Let F(x, y) be the curve and let (x, y) be a point on the curve.
We know the slope of the tangent to the curve at (x, y) is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex}
According to the given conditions, we get,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+5=x+y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x-5{/tex} 
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = -1 and Q = x - 5)
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int(-1) \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} .)=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F.}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=\int(\mathrm{x}-5) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, {tex}\int(\mathrm{x}-5) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=(\mathrm{x}-5) \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}-\int\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}-5) \cdot \int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}\right] \mathrm{dx}{/tex} 
= {tex}(x-5)\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}(x-5)\left(-e^{-x}\right)+\left(-e^{-x}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}(4-x) e^{-x}{/tex} 
Thus, from equation (i), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\mathrm{x}}=(4-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 - x + Cex
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y - 4 = Cex 
Now, it is given that curve passes through (0, 2).
Thus, equation (ii) becomes:
0 + 2 - 4 = C e0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 2 = C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -2
Substituting C = -2 in equation (ii), we get,
x + y - 4 =-2ex
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 - x - 2ex  
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
y = 4 - x - 2ex

question_text: The Integrating Factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=2 x^{2}{/tex} is
question_html: The Integrating Factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=2 x^{2}{/tex} is

options.explanation: It is given that {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=2 x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=2 x{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, {tex}p=-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x 2 ) Now, I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p d x}=e^{\int \frac{-1}{x} d x}=e^{\log \left(x^{-1}\right)}=x^{-1}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}

question_text: The Integrating Factor of the differential equation {tex}\left(1-y^{2}\right) \frac{d x}{d y}+y x=a y~(-1<y<1){/tex} is
question_html: The Integrating Factor of the differential equation {tex}\left(1-y^{2}\right) \frac{d x}{d y}+y x=a y~(-1<y<1){/tex} is

options.explanation: It is given that {tex}\left(1-y^{2}\right) \frac{d x}{d y}+y x=a y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{y x}{1-y^{2}}=\frac{a y}{1-y^{2}}{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p x=Q{/tex} (where {tex}p=\frac{y}{1-y^{2}}{/tex} and Q = {tex}\frac{ay}{1-y^{2}}{/tex} ) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{p} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{y}^{2}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-\mathrm{y}^{2}\right)}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{y}^{2}\right)}}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-y^{2}\right)}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+5 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-6 y=\log x{/tex}
question_html: Find the order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+5 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-6 y=\log x{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+5 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-6 y=\log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+5 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-6 y-\log x=0{/tex} We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} . Thus, its order is two. The highest power raised to {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is 1. Therefore, its degree is one.
solution_html:
It is given that equation is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+5 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-6 y=\log x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+5 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-6 y-\log x=0{/tex} 
We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}.
Thus, its order is two.
The highest power raised to {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is 1.
Therefore, its degree is one.

question_text: Find the order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+7 y=\sin x{/tex}
question_html: Find the order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+7 y=\sin x{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that equation is {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+7 y=\sin x{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+7 y-\sin x=0{/tex} We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} . Thus, its order is one. It is polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} . The highest power raised to {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} is 3. Therefore, its degree is three.
solution_html:
It is given that equation is {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+7 y=\sin x{/tex} 
{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+7 y-\sin x=0{/tex} 
We can see that the highest order derivative present in the differential is {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
Thus, its order is one. It is polynomial equation in {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}. The highest power raised to {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} is 3.
Therefore, its degree is three.

question_text: Verify that the function {tex}x y=a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+x^{2}{/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}-x y+x^{2}-2=0{/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function {tex}x y=a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+x^{2}{/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}-x y+x^{2}-2=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that xy = a e x + b e -x + x 2 Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow y+x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=a \cdot e^{x}-b e^{-x}+2 x{/tex} ...(i) Now, Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{d y}{d x}+x \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+2{/tex} ...(ii) Now, Using Equations. (i) and (ii), we get, LHS {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}-x y+x^{2}-2{/tex} = {tex}a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+2-\left[a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+x^{2}\right]+x^{2}-2{/tex} = {tex}\begin{aligned} &a e^{x}+b e^{x}+2 -a e^{x}-b e^{-x}-x^{2}+x^{2}-2 \end{aligned}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS = RHS. Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
solution_html:
It is given that xy = a ex + b e-x + x2
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow y+x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=a \cdot e^{x}-b e^{-x}+2 x{/tex}  ...(i)
Now, Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{d y}{d x}+x \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+2{/tex}   ...(ii)
Now, Using Equations. (i) and (ii), we get,
LHS  {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}-x y+x^{2}-2{/tex} 
= {tex}a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+2-\left[a e^{x}+b e^{-x}+x^{2}\right]+x^{2}-2{/tex}
= {tex}\begin{aligned} &a e^{x}+b e^{x}+2 -a e^{x}-b e^{-x}-x^{2}+x^{2}-2 \end{aligned}{/tex}
= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS = RHS.
Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.

question_text: Verify that the function {tex}y=e^{x}(a \cos x+b \sin x){/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=0{/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function {tex}y=e^{x}(a \cos x+b \sin x){/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that y = e x (a.cosx + bsinx) = a.e x cosx + b.e x sinx Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=a \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+b \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=a\left(e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right)+b \cdot\left(e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(a+b) e^{x} \cos x+(b-a) e^{x} \sin x{/tex} Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(a+b) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+(b-a) \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} = {tex}(a+b) \cdot\left[e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right]+(b-a)\left[e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right]{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{a.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}-\mathrm{a.cosx}]{/tex} = {tex}\left[2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx})\right]{/tex} Now, Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the given differential equations, we get, LHS = {tex}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y{/tex} = 2e x (b cos x - a sin x) -2e x [(a + b)cosx + (b - a ) sinx] + 2e x (a cos x + b sin x) =e x [(2b cos x - 2a sin x) - (2a cos x + 2b cosx) - (2b sinx - 2a sin x) + (2a cosx + 2b sin x)] = e x [(2b - 2a - 2b + 2a)cosx] + e x [(-2a - 2b + 2a + 2b) sinx] = 0 = RHS. Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
solution_html:
It is given that y = ex(a.cosx + bsinx) = a.excosx + b.exsinx 
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=a \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+b \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=a\left(e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right)+b \cdot\left(e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(a+b) e^{x} \cos x+(b-a) e^{x} \sin x{/tex} 
Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(a+b) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+(b-a) \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} 
= {tex}(a+b) \cdot\left[e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right]+(b-a)\left[e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{a.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}-\mathrm{a.cosx}]{/tex} 
= {tex}\left[2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx})\right]{/tex} 
Now, Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the given differential equations, we get,
LHS = {tex}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y{/tex} 
= 2ex(b cos x - a sin x) -2ex[(a + b)cosx + (b - a ) sinx] + 2ex(a cos x + b sin x)
=ex[(2b cos x - 2a sin x) - (2a cos x + 2b cosx) - (2b sinx - 2a sin x) + (2a cosx + 2b sin x)]
= ex[(2b - 2a - 2b + 2a)cosx] + ex[(-2a - 2b + 2a + 2b) sinx]
= 0 = RHS.
Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.

question_text: Verify that the function {tex}y=x \sin 3 x{/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+9 y-6 \cos 3 x=0{/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function {tex}y=x \sin 3 x{/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+9 y-6 \cos 3 x=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that y = x sin 3x Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x , we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(x \sin 3 x)=\sin 3 x+x . \cos 3 x .3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin 3 x+3 x \cos 3 x{/tex} Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}(\sin 3 x)+3 \frac{d}{d x}(x \cos 3 x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=3 \mathrm{cos} 3 \mathrm{x}+3[\cos 3 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}(-\sin 3 \mathrm{x}) \cdot 3]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=6 \cos 3 x-9 x \sin 3 x{/tex} Now, substituting the value of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the LHS of the given differential equation, we get, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+9 y-6 \cos 3 x{/tex} = (6.cos 3x - 9x sin 3x) + 9x sin 3x - 6 cos 3x = 0 = RHS Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
solution_html:
It is given that y = x sin 3x
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x , we get,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(x \sin 3 x)=\sin 3 x+x . \cos 3 x .3{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin 3 x+3 x \cos 3 x{/tex} 
Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}(\sin 3 x)+3 \frac{d}{d x}(x \cos 3 x){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=3 \mathrm{cos} 3 \mathrm{x}+3[\cos 3 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}(-\sin 3 \mathrm{x}) \cdot 3]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=6 \cos 3 x-9 x \sin 3 x{/tex} 
Now, substituting the value of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the LHS of the given differential equation, we get,
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+9 y-6 \cos 3 x{/tex} 
= (6.cos 3x - 9x sin 3x) + 9x sin 3x - 6 cos 3x
= 0 = RHS
Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.

question_text: Verify that the function {tex}x^{2}=2 y^{2} \log y{/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}-x y=0{/tex}
question_html: Verify that the function {tex}x^{2}=2 y^{2} \log y{/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}-x y=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that x 2 = 2y 2 log y Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}2 x=2 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(y^{2} \log y\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\left[2 \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\mathrm{y}^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{y}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}(2 \mathrm{ylogy}+\mathrm{y}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x}{y(1+2 \log y)}{/tex} Now, substituting the value of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} in the LHS of the given differential equation, we get, {tex}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\mathrm{xy}=\left(2 \mathrm{y}^{2} \log \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{y}^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}(1+2 \log \mathrm{y})}-\mathrm{xy}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{y}^{2}(1+2 \log \mathrm{y}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}(1+2 \log \mathrm{y})}-\mathrm{xy}{/tex} = xy - xy = 0 Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
solution_html:
It is given that x2 = 2y2 log y
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}2 x=2 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(y^{2} \log y\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\left[2 \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\mathrm{y}^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{y}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}(2 \mathrm{ylogy}+\mathrm{y}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x}{y(1+2 \log y)}{/tex} 
Now, substituting the value of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} in the LHS of the given differential equation, we get,
{tex}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\mathrm{xy}=\left(2 \mathrm{y}^{2} \log \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{y}^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}(1+2 \log \mathrm{y})}-\mathrm{xy}{/tex} 
= {tex}\mathrm{y}^{2}(1+2 \log \mathrm{y}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}(1+2 \log \mathrm{y})}-\mathrm{xy}{/tex} 
= xy - xy
= 0
Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
