question_text: Prove that x 2 - y 2 = c (x 2 + y 2 ) 2 is the general solution of differential equation {tex}(x^3-3xy^2)dx=(y^3-3x^2y)dy{/tex} , where c is a parameter.
question_html: Prove that x2 - y2 = c (x2 + y2)2 is the general solution of differential equation {tex}(x^3-3xy^2)dx=(y^3-3x^2y)dy{/tex}, where c is a parameter.
solution_text: Given differential equation can be rewritten as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x y ^ { 2 } } { y ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }y }{/tex} ...(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation, so, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} Then, Eq. (i) becomes {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ( v x ) ^ { 2 } } { ( v x ) ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } ( v x ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } - v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 4 } + 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v - 3 \int \frac { v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v = \log x + \log C{/tex} ...(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow - \frac{1}{4}\log \left( {1 - {v^4}} \right) - \frac{3}{4}\log \left| {\frac{{1 + {v^2}}}{{1 - {v^2}}}} \right| = \log x + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 4 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) \times \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 1 / 4 } = \log Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = ( C x ) ^ { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = v x ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [taking square root] {tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} (where C 1 = C {tex}^2{/tex} ) .
solution_html:
Given  differential equation can be rewritten as
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x y ^ { 2 } } { y ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }y }{/tex} ...(i)
This is a homogeneous differential equation, so, put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
Then, Eq. (i) becomes
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ( v x ) ^ { 2 } } { ( v x ) ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } ( v x ) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } - v{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 4 } + 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v - 3 \int \frac { v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v = \log x + \log C{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow - \frac{1}{4}\log \left( {1 - {v^4}} \right) - \frac{3}{4}\log \left| {\frac{{1 + {v^2}}}{{1 - {v^2}}}} \right| = \log x + \log C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 4 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) \times \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 1 / 4 } = \log Cx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = ( C x ) ^ { - 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = v x ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [taking square root]
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}(where C1 = C{tex}^2{/tex}).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{y}^{2}}}=-\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} On integrating, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin -1 y + sin -1 x = C
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{y}^{2}}}=-\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} 
On integrating, we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin-1y + sin-1x = C

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y^{2}+y+1}{x^{2}+x+1}=0{/tex} is given by (x + y + 1) = A (1 - x - y - 2xy), where A is parameter.
question_html: Show that the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y^{2}+y+1}{x^{2}+x+1}=0{/tex} is given by (x + y + 1) = A (1 - x - y - 2xy), where A is parameter.
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y^{2}+y+1}{x^{2}+x+1}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{y^{2}+y+1}{x^{2}+x+1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y^{2}+y+1}=\frac{-d x}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y^{2}+y+1}+\frac{d x}{x^{2}+x+1}=0{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac{d y}{y^{2}+y+1}+\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+x+1}=c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{\left(y+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}+\int \frac{d y}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}=c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}+\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\right]+\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\right]=\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]=C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}}{1-\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+2}{\sqrt{3}}}{1-\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{xy}+2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+1}{3}\right)}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{3-4 x y-2 x-2 y-1}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{2(1-x-y-2 x y)}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{(1-x-y-2 x y)}=\tan \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} c\right){/tex} Let {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C\right)=B{/tex} Then {tex}\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1=\frac{2 \mathrm{B}}{\sqrt{3}}(1-\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}-2 \mathrm{xy}){/tex} Now let {tex}A=\frac{2 B}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} then, we have, x + y + 1 = A(1 - x - y - 2xy)
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y^{2}+y+1}{x^{2}+x+1}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{y^{2}+y+1}{x^{2}+x+1}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y^{2}+y+1}=\frac{-d x}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y^{2}+y+1}+\frac{d x}{x^{2}+x+1}=0{/tex} 
On integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{y^{2}+y+1}+\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+x+1}=c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{\left(y+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}+\int \frac{d y}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}=c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}+\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\right]+\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}\right]=\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]=C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}}{1-\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+2}{\sqrt{3}}}{1-\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{xy}+2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+1}{3}\right)}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{3-4 x y-2 x-2 y-1}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{2(1-x-y-2 x y)}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{(1-x-y-2 x y)}=\tan \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} c\right){/tex} 
Let {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} C\right)=B{/tex} 
Then {tex}\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1=\frac{2 \mathrm{B}}{\sqrt{3}}(1-\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}-2 \mathrm{xy}){/tex} 
Now let {tex}A=\frac{2 B}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} then, we have,
x + y + 1 = A(1 - x - y - 2xy)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the equation of the curve passing through the point {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} whose differential equation is {tex}\sin x\cos ydx + \cos x\sin ydy = 0{/tex}
question_html: Find the equation of the curve passing through the point {tex}\left( {0,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}whose differential equation is {tex}\sin x\cos ydx + \cos x\sin ydy = 0{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\sin x.\cos ydx = - \cos x.\sin ydy{/tex} {tex}\frac{sin x}{cos x}dx = - \frac{sin y}{cos y} dy {/tex} {tex}\int {\tan xdx = - \int {\tan y\,dy} } {/tex} {tex}\log \left( {\sec x} \right) = - \log \left( {\sec y} \right) + \log c{/tex} {tex}\log \left( {\sec x.\sec y} \right) = \log c{/tex} {tex}\sec x.\sec y = c{/tex} ......(i) When {tex}x = 0,y= \pi /4{/tex} {tex}c = \sqrt 2 {/tex} put the value of c in eq (i) {tex}\sec x.\sec y = \sqrt 2 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\sin x.\cos ydx = - \cos x.\sin ydy{/tex}

{tex}\frac{sin x}{cos x}dx = - \frac{sin y}{cos y} dy {/tex}

{tex}\int {\tan xdx = - \int {\tan y\,dy} } {/tex}

{tex}\log \left( {\sec x} \right) = - \log \left( {\sec y} \right) + \log c{/tex}

{tex}\log \left( {\sec x.\sec y} \right) = \log c{/tex}

{tex}\sec x.\sec y = c{/tex} ......(i)

When {tex}x = 0,y= \pi /4{/tex}

{tex}c = \sqrt 2 {/tex}

put the value of c in eq (i)

{tex}\sec x.\sec y = \sqrt 2 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (1 + e 2x )dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (1 + e2x)dy + (1 + y2)ex dx = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
solution_text: Given differential equation is, (1 + e 2x )dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0 Above equation may be written as {tex}\frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = - \int \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex} On putting e x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e x dx = dt in RHS, we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } t + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) + C{/tex} ...(i) [put t = e x ] Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0. On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get tan -1 1 = -tan -1 (e 0 ) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 }1 = - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 2 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } e ^ { x } + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \tan \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right] = \cot \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cot \left[ \cot ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } } \right) \right] \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \cot ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,

(1 + e2x)dy + (1 + y2)ex dx = 0
Above equation may be written as
{tex}\frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = - \int \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex}
On putting ex = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ex dx = dt in RHS, we get
{tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } t + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) + C{/tex} ...(i) [put t = ex]
Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get
tan-11 = -tan-1(e0) + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 }1 = - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 2 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting {tex}C = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } e ^ { x } + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \tan \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right] = \cot \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \cot \left[ \cot ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } } \right) \right] \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \cot ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y(y \neq 0){/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y(y \neq 0){/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i) Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get, {tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii) From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy On integrating both sides, we get, z = y + C {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i)
Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} 
Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get,
{tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii)
From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get,
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy
On integrating both sides, we get,
z = y + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation (x - y) (dx + dy) = dx - dy, given that y = -1, when x = 0.
question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation (x - y) (dx + dy) = dx - dy, given that y = -1, when x = 0.
solution_text: It is given that (x - y)(dx + dy) = dx - dy ⇒ (x - y + 1)dy = (1 - x + y)dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x+y}{x-y+1}{/tex} ......(i) Let x – y = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x} (x-y)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} Now, let us substitute the value of x-y and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} in equation (i), we get, {tex}1-\frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{1-t}{1+t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=1-\left(\frac{1-t}{1+t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{(1+t)-(1-t)}{1+t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{2 t}{1+t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1+t}{t}\right) d t=2 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{1}{t}\right) d t=2 d x{/tex} .....(ii) On integrating both side, we get, t + log|t| = 2x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ( x - y) + log |x - y| = 2x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|x – y| = x + y + C .......(iii) Now, y = -1 at x = 0 Then, equation (iii), we get, log 1 = 0 - 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Substituting C = 1 in equation (iii), we get, log|x - y| = x + y + 1 Therefore, a particular solution of the given differential equation is log|x - y| = x + y + 1.
solution_html:
It is given that (x - y)(dx + dy) = dx - dy
⇒ (x - y + 1)dy = (1 - x + y)dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-x+y}{x-y+1}{/tex}  ......(i) 
Let x – y = t
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x} (x-y)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} 
Now, let us substitute the value of x-y and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} in equation (i), we get, 
{tex}1-\frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{1-t}{1+t}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=1-\left(\frac{1-t}{1+t}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{(1+t)-(1-t)}{1+t}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{2 t}{1+t}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1+t}{t}\right) d t=2 d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{1}{t}\right) d t=2 d x{/tex} .....(ii)
On integrating both side, we get,
t + log|t| = 2x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ( x - y) + log |x - y| = 2x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|x – y| = x + y + C .......(iii)
Now, y = -1 at x = 0
Then, equation (iii), we get,
log 1 = 0 - 1 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Substituting C = 1 in equation (iii), we get,
log|x - y| = x + y + 1
Therefore, a particular solution of the given differential equation is log|x - y| = x + y + 1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 (x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0)
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 (x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0)
solution_text: {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex} Given differential equation is of the form: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex} {tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx} }} = {e^{2\sqrt x }}{/tex} Solution is, {tex}y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {{e^{2\sqrt x }} \times \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}dx + c}{/tex} {tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx + C} {/tex} {tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = 2\sqrt x + C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex}
Given differential equation is of the form:
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex}
{tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx} }} = {e^{2\sqrt x }}{/tex}
Solution is,
{tex}y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {{e^{2\sqrt x }} \times \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}dx + c}{/tex}
{tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx + C} {/tex}
{tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = 2\sqrt x + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex} , x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex}, x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
solution_text: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = cot x and Q = 4x cosec x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } \mathrm { x } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \log | x | } = x \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \times \sin x = \int 4 x\ cosec x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \sin x d x + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} . On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} 0 = 2 \times \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex} C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = 2 x ^ { 2 } \ cosec x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \; cosec \; x{/tex} [dividing both sides by sin x] which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here P = cot x and Q = 4x cosec x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } \mathrm { x } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \log | x | } = x \right]{/tex}
The solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \times \sin x = \int 4 x\ cosec x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \sin x d x + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} 0 = 2 \times \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting {tex} C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = 2 x ^ { 2 } \ cosec x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \; cosec \; x{/tex} [dividing both sides by sin x]
which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
solution_text: It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = -dt Substituting value in equation (i), we get, {tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - e y | = log|C(x + 1)| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii) Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} 
On integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i)
Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} eydy = -dt
Substituting value in equation (i), we get,
{tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - ey| = log|C(x + 1)|
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii)
Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} 
Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}

question_text: The general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{y d x-x d y}{y}=0{/tex} is
question_html: The general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{y d x-x d y}{y}=0{/tex} is

options.explanation: It is given that {tex}\frac{\mathrm{ydx}-\mathrm{xdy}}{\mathrm{y}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{ydx}-\mathrm{xdy}}{\mathrm{xy}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x} d x-\frac{1}{y} d y=0{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get, log|x| - log|y| = log k {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{x}{y}\right|=\log k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = Cx where C = {tex}\frac{1}{k}{/tex}

question_text: The general solution of a differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} is
question_html: The general solution of a differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} is

options.text: {tex}y e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}=\int\left(\mathrm{Q}_{1} e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}\right) d y+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
options.html: {tex}y e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}=\int\left(\mathrm{Q}_{1} e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}\right) d y+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

options.text: {tex}y \cdot e^{\int_{P} d x}=\int\left(Q_{1} e^{\int P_{1} d x}\right) d x+C{/tex}
options.html: {tex}y \cdot e^{\int_{P} d x}=\int\left(Q_{1} e^{\int P_{1} d x}\right) d x+C{/tex}

options.text: {tex}x e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}=\int\left(\mathrm{Q}_{1} e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}\right) d y+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
options.html: {tex}x e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}=\int\left(\mathrm{Q}_{1} e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}\right) d y+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
options.explanation: The integrating factor of the given differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dy}}+\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{Q}_{1} \text { is } \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{/tex} Thus, the general solution of the differential equation is given by, {tex}\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{I.F.})=\int(\mathrm{Q_1} \times \mathrm{I.F.}) \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} . \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}=\int\left(\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

options.text: {tex}x e^{\int P^{1} d x}=\int\left(Q_{1} e^{\int P_{1} d x}\right) d x+C{/tex}
options.html: {tex}x e^{\int P^{1} d x}=\int\left(Q_{1} e^{\int P_{1} d x}\right) d x+C{/tex}

correct_option_text: {tex}x e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}=\int\left(\mathrm{Q}_{1} e^{\int \mathrm{P}_{1} d y}\right) d y+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

question_text: The general solution of the differential equation e x dy + (y e x + 2x) dx = 0 is
question_html: The general solution of the differential equation ex dy + (y ex + 2x) dx = 0 is

options.explanation: It is given that e x dy + (ye x + 2x) dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\mathrm{ye}^{\mathrm{x}}+2 \mathrm{x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+y=-2 x e^{-x}{/tex} This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = 1 and Q = -2xe -x ) Now, I.F = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}y(I . F .)=\int(Q \times I . F .) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{\mathrm{x}}=\int\left(-2 \mathrm{xe}^{-\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{\mathrm{x}}=-\int 2 \mathrm{xdx}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye x = -x 2 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye x + x 2 = C
